I can’t believe I forgot to text Sarah back. I mean like, how hard is it to remember a quick response? It's not like my phone is ever really away from me. Didn't she ask me about the party this weekend? I should probably reply before she thinks I’m ignoring her. Ugh, I hope she hasn’t texted someone else. It’s ridiculous how much I care about what other people think. I mean, it drives me crazy sometimes but then again I need to keep my social life alive. Okay, so last weekend at Jake’s birthday—oh my god, that was a scene. The energy was wild, tons of people everywhere, and I loved it. But then there's always that one person who gets too drunk, right? Like, why can’t people just chill? It’s a party, not a wrestling match. I was laughing but also like, do I really want this chaos right now? I spent half the night making sure no one spilled their drink on my shoes. Those are my favorite shoes! They’re like navy blue but when the light shines they kinda look sparkly. I don’t know why people get wasted like it’s cool. Don’t people have better things to do? I mean, it makes me anxious just thinking about it. But then again, I feed off the energy. I love talking to new people, making connections, but some of them, like, what’s wrong with them? I mean, why would you come up to me just to share your conspiracy theory about birds? I get it, unique perspective or whatever, but can we please just talk about something normal? It’s a party, it’s supposed to be fun! I’d rather talk about someone’s new tattoo or the latest Netflix show everyone’s binging. At least that’s relatable. What’s with people being nice all the time too? I don’t get it. It's like they think everyone has to be pals or something. I’m not your pal if you just walk in and start chatting with me. I get it; community is important, but like, can we pick and choose our battles? Sometimes I just want to be alone in a crowd, watch the circus unfold and not have to be part of it. I’m not here to make everyone feel warm and fuzzy. I mostly just want to hear the gossip and maybe take a couple of selfies for Instagram. And then there are those friends who say, “Oh, let’s all be friendly!” Like, no thanks, Karen. I’d rather just hang out with the people I actually like. Then there’s the whole planning aspect. Who even has time for plans? It’s so tedious. We’re all just bouncing from one thing to another, and if I have to plan one more dinner I’m going to lose my mind. Why does it have to be a group decision? Just go without me if you can’t figure it out. I don’t want to sit there and debate about where to eat for three hours. Just pick a place! I like spontaneity, but too many plans drain me, like, sorry guys, can’t commit. I just want to have fun, but all this organization is just too much. And then after all of that, I can’t help but think about the fallout. What if I said something dumb, you know? Every time I think I’m being funny, I question it later. Did I just embarrass myself? Like, what did I say? I can’t even remember why I said certain things, and it haunts me later like why did you even bring that up? Why can’t I just be chill like everyone else? Is it too much to ask to be carefree without worrying about how I come off? The other day, I tripped in class while trying to get to my seat. And of course, everyone looked at me, and all I could think was how I probably looked like a total fool. I laughed it off, but inside I was flaming with embarrassment. A part of me was okay with the attention but the other part wanted to melt into the floor. Why do we care so much what everyone thinks? Just be you, right? But being me is exhausting sometimes because I’m always worried about being judged. I’m not even that great at keeping a straight face when I’m anxious, which doesn’t help in group settings. And why do I even care what people think? I can be funny, spontaneous, and I feel like I have a lot to contribute. But then there’s this voice in my head that says, “Just don’t screw it up.” And now I’m thinking about Sarah again. I should probably text her. I’ll just say something quick, like “Hey, sorry for the delay! I’d love to come to the party!” And hopefully, no one has killed the vibe in the meantime. I just need to get my shoes on and be ready for whatever comes next: chaos, laughter, and all the craziness of being young and social.

7:34 PM and I’m thinking about how I really should start doing some homework. The weather is perfect though, not too hot, kind of breezy, and the thought of sitting inside to study feels like a sin against nature. Why do I always procrastinate? Maybe it’s all the distractions. My phone buzzes and I can’t help but check it, just the group chat blowing up again about something that feels so trivial but also kind of funny. I laugh at a meme but then I just can’t shake that nagging feeling in my stomach like I should be doing something productive instead of scrolling. Why is it so hard to be motivated? Sometimes I wonder if it’s because I care too much. I mean, I want to do well for myself and for others, like my family, my professors. It’s overwhelming sometimes, trying to balance this need to please everyone while I can’t even please myself, like, I don’t know how to say no without feeling guilty. It’s like I’m stuck in this weird cycle of wanting to be liked and wanting to be free at the same time. Can you ever get to that balance? I get anxious when I think about disappointing someone, but then I also crave solitude. It’s like my brain is at war. That reminds me of this time in high school when I just froze on stage during a presentation. I thought I rehearsed enough, but then when I got up there, my mind just blanked. Everyone staring at me. My heart was pounding. I wanted to run away but, well, I didn’t. I just stood there awkwardly for what felt like an eternity, then I stumbled through the rest and it was all this awful blur. I could feel my face getting hot and I knew I was probably turning bright red. I got through it but the laughs later from some classmates cut deep. I never wanted to present again. Wait, why am I even thinking about that? It’s done. I mean, isn’t that the point of all those motivational quotes? “Face your fears!” or whatever. I read somewhere that that kind of moment builds character or resilience or something. But honestly, can it just build anxiety instead? I feel like that’s what I got out of it. I’d rather be in a room with the same two or three people that I just click with, sharing our weird thoughts and laughing at inside jokes than trying to be that person who everyone loves. Sometimes I feel like I wear my thoughts and feelings on my sleeve a little bit too much, and it’s exhausting. Anyway, I really want to be creative right now. I have this urge to paint or draw, but I have zero talent in that area. I admire people who can create things that make you feel something. It feels like magic to me. Sometimes I scribble in my notebook, just random shapes and lines that don’t really make sense, but they feel like they mean something to me. I think that’s why I love exploring new music and different cultures—like just diving into something entirely different and feeling the emotions that come with it. I find myself listening to world music on repeat, just letting those sounds wash over me. It’s calming, but I don’t know how to articulate why. Oh, speaking of music, I should really be studying for that exam coming up, but, ugh, there’s this pressure. With all the material I need to go over, and I haven’t even gotten through half of it yet. I’ll probably pull an all-nighter. Again. Which never turns out well. I jolt awake at random times and do that awful panic wake-up where your heart is racing and you question all your life choices—like, why didn’t I just start earlier? It’s the cycle again. I really wish I could be that person who just tackles things head-on, you know? The one who has it all figured out. Instead, I’m just here, a bundle of feelings and thoughts and half-finished plans. I mean, I’ve been reading this existential book that makes me question everything, but do I even have the right to feel this way? I get so lost in my head. I want to connect with people but the idea of small talk just feels so tedious. And people are busy, I get that, but why is it that every time I reach out, I feel like I’m intruding? Maybe that’s why I get caught in these loops of contemplation. I feel like I’m always waiting for something to happen but not really taking charge of my own life. I just overthink every little thing. It’s a wonder I even managed to get out of bed sometimes. But then again, there’s this wild part of me that craves experiences, new ideas, the unknown. How do I balance it all? I guess that’s the real question here. Time’s racing, and I’m still just trying to make sense of everything. But isn’t that what it’s all about?

So I was thinking about how the old wooden chair creaks when I sit in it, the sound blending into the background noise of my thoughts. I should probably fix it. But then again, fixing it means I would have to get up and find the tools I don’t even know if I have and then there's the chance I might hurt myself with the screwdriver or something. Focus. What was I saying? Ah, it’s interesting how some people might see that chair and think it’s a piece of junk, but I like it. It’s comfy. It has stories. I wonder who sat in it before me. Maybe someone had deep conversations in it, or maybe just daydreamed like I do. I really like daydreaming. The other day I thought about a road trip to nowhere. Just driving and driving, seeing where the road leads, music playing loudly and the windows rolled down. There’s something freeing about the open road, isn’t there? But I’d probably just end up at some random diner talking to a stranger about their life. I think I’d like that. Just connecting with people, hearing their stories. Speaking of stories, I love that new podcast I found — it’s about unusual friendships. It’s amazing how two people can come from totally different worlds and find common ground. I find that fascinating, how people can bond over the strangest things. Like, you wouldn’t think someone who loves collecting stamps and a thrill-seeker who jumps out of planes could be friends, but they find a way. I feel like I can see that in everyone — the connections we have, even when they seem superficial. Everyone has layers, right? I want to peel those layers back and see what’s underneath, but sometimes, I get overwhelmed by the idea of reaching out. What if they don’t want to share? Then I think, whatever. I’d just move on. No biggie. Isn’t it weird how we can feel so different around different people? Like, I often feel like a complete introvert around a group, but with one-on-one conversations, it’s like I’m totally at ease. I guess it depends on the vibe. Vibes are important. I like to think I’m pretty agreeable; I want to make people feel comfortable. If someone’s upset, my instinct is to just sit there and listen, to help them open up. But sometimes, I feel like I’m too laid-back. I mean, it can be a bit daunting when everyone is rushing and I’m just there, enjoying the moment. Like, who cares if I haven’t done that assignment yet? Some people freak out over deadlines. Not me. They can keep their stress, I’m just going to chill. But I’m curious. I want to know what it’s like to be that stressed, to have that urgency. Maybe it’s exciting in a way? I guess it could also lead to burnout. My roommate is like that, always in overdrive. I honestly admire it a little, even if it exhausts me to watch. I can sit there with tea, just observing as they run from one thing to the next. I wonder what their inner world is like — are they constantly racing? I hope they find moments like I do when everything slows down, moments where they can just breathe. Then there’s my friend who’s really into art. She sees colors and shapes I just don’t notice. She gets lost in her work and comes out with these incredible pieces that make me feel things I can’t even articulate. I wish I could tap into that creativity. I love how art can tell stories without words. It makes me think of how I always find new interests. I could be obsessed with photography one month and then be knee-deep in a cooking phase the next. Open to everything, I guess. But I don’t have the discipline sometimes to follow through. That’s probably where the low conscientiousness rating comes in. It can be a bit frustrating because I want to try everything, but then I just flit from one thing to another and never settle. I wonder how that feels for other people with high conscientiousness, sticking with one thing and diving deep. Is it fulfilling? Do they ever feel like they’re missing out? I wonder if they ever just want to drop everything and go on an adventure or explore a new hobby without worrying about finishing what they started. But maybe that’s just me. I find joy in the possibilities of things, not necessarily in the completion. Sometimes I feel like an outsider looking in, watching everyone hustle and bustle, and I’m over here enjoying the little things, like how sunlight hits a leaf or how nice it is to feel grass under my feet. Simple joys, but they fill me up in ways that I can’t explain. And I guess that’s okay. Even if I’m not fitting into a mold, I’m learning to appreciate the world around me as it is, not as it should be.

I can’t help but think about how I always feel slightly out of sync with everyone else. Like in a party, all these faces laughing and talking, and me just standing there, looking for an escape route or a cozy corner where I can disappear. I’d much rather be at home with a stack of books and a playlist that no one else would appreciate. And when I start thinking about social gatherings, I can feel that anxiety creeping in—what if I say the wrong thing, what if people stare? It’s exhausting to navigate. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just wired differently. I’m so curious about everything, which is a blessing, I guess but it also feels like a curse when I see everyone else connecting and I’m analyzing the way the light hits the glass on the table instead of joining the conversation. I remember this one class discussion where the professor was talking about the concept of ‘emergent properties,’ and I just lost myself in thought about how that applies to everything in life. Random collections of neurons firing in my brain, leading to thoughts and feelings that overwhelm me or inspire me. I’m so aware of the chaotic swirl of ideas in my head, and it feels like I’m being pulled in a hundred directions all at once. I could talk about that for hours but when it comes to small talk, I just can’t do it. I have this incessant urge to dive deep into topics no one wants to discuss. Like the philosophical implications of modern technology or the way art can reflect society. But people don’t want to go there, they want to talk about the weather or the latest show on Netflix. Ugh, social etiquette is such a weird concept. Why do we pretend everything's fine when the world is such a hot mess? I want to shake people and say, “Can’t you see the absurdity?” But then I know I won’t do that. I’d rather fade into the background or get lost in my phone. My phone has become my comfort zone, a way to escape those awkward moments. Scrolling through social media feeds, I see everyone’s perfect lives, and there’s this pit that forms in my stomach—does everyone feel this way? Or am I just the odd one out again? And then there’s the whole trust issue. I mean, how do people just let their guards down? I can’t remember the last time I felt completely at ease around someone. It’s as if my brain is stuck replaying all the times I’ve been let down or misunderstood. I wish I could just turn that part of me off. It’s like I’m watching a movie with a really critical commentary playing in my head. The nuances of body language and tone, over-analyzing everything. It’s a wonder I ever feel relaxed. I can’t shake the feeling that everyone expects so much from me. I stumble through assignments, barely scraping by, and yet I’m constantly bombarded with reminders of what I should be doing. I see my classmates acing their work, and I occasionally feel this flicker of envy. How do they manage their time? What’s the secret sauce? I would rather explore ideas than compile the perfect essay. I’m tired of feeling like I’m not reaching any potential but the minute anyone suggests structure or routine, I run for the hills. I wonder if everyone has these spirals of thoughts. Maybe people just hide it better. It’d be nice to think we’re all in the same boat, but then there’s that nagging voice saying I’m just different. I think about traveling, how many places I haven’t seen, how much art I haven’t experienced. I wish I could just pack a bag and go. No plans, just freedom, but the idea of doing that alone is intimidating. Would I cry in the airport? Get lost in a foreign city? I daydream about the feeling of landing somewhere new, everywhere smelling different, tasting different. But then what about the people? How do I talk to them? What if I can’t connect? Maybe I’m just meant for a different kind of experience. I feel like I see the world in a way that’s unique, that’s for sure. It’s just the getting there that’s the problem. I can muse about grand ideas but struggle with simple interactions. There’s so much pressure to be someone I’m not. I want to break free but I’m stuck in my comfort zone, trapped by my own thoughts. And sometimes, just sometimes, it feels like the weight of existence gets heavy. I can’t help but wonder how to navigate through all of that. There’s beauty in chaos, isn’t there? Still, I can't shake the nagging sense of unease. So I keep my head down, hoping to figure it all out one day.

Okay so I just got back from that group project meeting and wow, it was chaotic. Everyone had different ideas, which is good, I guess, but my head is spinning trying to keep track of it all. I mean, I like working with people, I really do, but sometimes I wish they’d just stick to the plan. Why can’t we just follow the outline I sent? It’s like herding cats. But I guess that’s part of being in a team, right? Collaboration and all that. I like to think I keep everything organized and on track. Maybe I should’ve printed out a schedule or something. I should’ve done that. I hate when things get messy. Messy makes me anxious, and I definitely don’t need more of that right now. My mind just races, thinking about all the things I have to do. And speaking of anxiety, I can’t stop thinking about that presentation next week. I know I’ll get through it, but my stomach is already in knots. I hate that feeling. It creeps up on me like an unwelcome guest. What if I trip over my words? What if they don’t like my ideas? I really want to impress the professor. I’ve put so much work into this. I can’t have my face turn beet red in front of everyone. People tell me I do well under pressure, but I can’t shake that nagging voice. It’s exhausting just thinking about it. I need to practice more, maybe in front of a mirror. I should probably call Sarah later. She always calms me down. She’s so nice and understanding. I wish I could be more like her sometimes. Why do I always overthink everything? I mean, even the small stuff, like what to wear to class. I spent an hour picking out an outfit today. Is that normal? I just want to look put together, but sometimes I feel like I’m trying too hard. Maybe I should just wear t-shirts and jeans, but I don’t know if that would be enough. I guess I care too much about what others think. But what’s wrong with wanting to look nice? The weather is awful today, gloomy and gray, which isn’t helping my mood. Do I really have to go out later? I could just stay in and binge-watch something. But I wouldn’t want to miss hanging out with the others. What if they plan something fun without me? I hate missing out on plans. So many FOMO moments lately. I sometimes feel obligated to join in because I want to be a good friend. And I want them to see how excited I am, even if I’m nervous inside. It’s just too much at times. But every time I’m around them, I feel this rush of energy. I guess that’s the good part of being extroverted. I thrive in social situations, even if they make me jittery. Okay, I have to study for that exam too. Can’t forget that. I’ve been keeping a detailed schedule, breaking down the chapters, but I just can’t seem to focus. My mind wanders off to random thoughts, like what I’ll eat for dinner. Pasta? Pizza? I love food. So many options, which sounds great until I have to decide. I tend to stick to what I know. I mean, it’s practical, right? Why try exotic food when I can just grab a sandwich? I don’t want to end up hating dinner. Just want something reliable. I know it’s a bit boring. I wish I could be more adventurous like other people but that thought makes me uneasy. I need to remember to text Jess too. She’s been down lately, and I want to check in. I hate seeing my friends upset. It makes me sad, and I’ve been feeling kind of on edge lately. I hope she’s okay. I always worry about my friends. I just want to make sure they’re feeling alright. I feel like it’s my responsibility to be there for them. That makes me feel good, but sometimes it becomes overwhelming. How do I balance that with everything else? I’m such a people pleaser, and while it makes me happy to help, sometimes I wish I could just take a step back and not worry. Okay, enough thinking about everyone else. What do I want to do? Maybe hit the gym later. That always helps me clear my head. I feel so accomplished after a good workout, and it helps with the anxiety. I always wear my favorite shoes when I go. Such a small comfort but it works. I just get so caught up in my thoughts, it’s exhausting but that’s who I am. I’ll get through it. I always do. Just need to keep everything sorted, one step at a time. A plan, that’s my mantra.

I keep thinking about how the weather feels too unpredictable lately, like my moods. One minute I’m okay, and then suddenly I’m caught in a downpour of anxiety about everything. The laundry, deadlines, social interactions, even what to eat for dinner. I really hate small talk, especially with people I don’t know well. It's exhausting. Sometimes I’d rather just stare at my phone and pretend I’m busy. I mean, does anyone actually enjoy it? I don’t get how people can jump from topic to topic so easily. My brain feels like a tangled mess of thoughts rather than something coherent. It’s like I’m stuck on a loop sometimes, replaying the same awkward conversation that happened three weeks ago. There’s this guy in my class who keeps asking me group project stuff, and oh my god I just want to disappear. Why can’t he pick someone else? Right, the project due next week. I haven’t even started the research, and here I am worrying about how I’m going to respond to him when he inevitably messages me later. I wonder if he thinks I’m unfriendly or something. I can be rude, I guess. It’s not like I mean to be; I just don’t know how to fake it. What's wrong with being honest though? Everyone else seems to be living in some perfect social bubble, and I’m just floating outside it, like a balloon that got free from the kids’ birthday party. Do people around here even care to know me? Probably not. It gets so tiring, pretending I’m okay with everybody’s jokes about the same things, like who’s dating whom or whatever. I don’t care. And speaking of not caring, my roommate keeps leaving dirty dishes in the sink. I thought we had an agreement about cleaning up after ourselves. I’m definitely not taking the bait and cleaning her stuff. I’m just going to let it stack up until she gets sick of looking at it. Am I a bad person for thinking that? It honestly feels good to think about her cleaning it up; that makes me feel a little less anxious, but it’s not going to inspire any great friendship here. I went to the library yesterday, which was a decision I regretted instantly. It’s supposed to be a quiet place, but it was like a social hub. People talking like it’s a café or something—fingers tapping, whispers, laughter, I couldn’t concentrate. I ended up sitting in the far corner and staring at my phone for an hour. I didn’t even realize it until I glanced at the time, and I had lost an hour of precious research time. I thought try to enjoy the strange art on the walls while I was there, but it just ended up making me feel more out of place. I should’ve just gone home, but home feels so isolating too. It’s like a trap sometimes. Those walls close in and all I hear is the noise in my head. Then there’s this ongoing debate in my mind about whether I should try to connect with people or just keep it all at arm’s length. I daydream about being more social, but the reality is always a comedown. That party last week? I stayed for less than 30 minutes. I just couldn’t take the chatter and the forced smiles. It’s like everyone is on this wavelength that I haven’t figured out yet. I drift through conversations but feel like I’m hovering outside my body. I remember I caught a glimpse of my reflection in the bathroom mirror, and I looked more tired than I ever had. I ended up in a corner scrolling Twitter instead, watching my peers enjoy themselves. And I don’t even want to think about dating—oh god, what a hassle. The thought of going on a date sends me spiraling. I’d probably overthink every little thing. Does he like this? Is my laugh weird? Do I look okay? Why can’t there be an app that just pairs you with someone based on mutual disdain for socializing? I’d swipe right every single time. Is that a rule, or is it just me wanting a loophole in social structures? Is it crazy that I long for deep conversations about the universe but can’t bring myself to engage in basic chit-chat? Sometimes I wish I could bottle up all my thoughts and just share them in one big go instead of doing this back-and-forth dance. I mean, there’s so much going on in my head, but it feels like I’m constantly filtering, deleting, and rewriting scripts that no one will read. Why is it so hard to just be? I think about how freeing it’d be, to just say everything on my mind without worrying about being judged. It’s exhausting to hold it all in, but isn’t it better to stay quiet sometimes? I don’t know. I just want to feel understood without having to go through the motions, without feeling like I’ve got to put on a mask every day.

I really don’t get people sometimes, it’s like they’re all living in some sort of bubble wrapped in sunshine and smiles while I’m over here just picking at everything. I really hate small talk, like what even is the point of asking someone how their weekend was if you don’t actually care? Just a bunch of pleasantries that mean nothing—what's the deal with that? I’d rather dive into something real, you know? But then I remember that’s probably why I struggle so much with groups. I mean, I try to engage, but my mind just ends up racing through a million what-ifs that I can’t keep straight. Like, is this person judging me? Do they actually think I’m boring? Probably, right? So anyway, I was at this party last week. Yeah, I don’t really go to parties, but a friend dragged me along. I mean dragged, because I had a million excuses ready to bail. They finally convinced me. So I got there, and the vibe was all wrong. Music blasting, people laughing, and I’m over in the corner trying to look nonchalant but really, I’m just freaking out inside. Like, what’s everyone talking about? What do I even say? I can’t just jump in because I don’t want to be that weird person who interrupts and then nobody wants to talk to me. So I just sat there nursing my drink, watching people. It’s fascinating and terrifying. They seem so confident, so sure of themselves. I wish I could be like that, but then I think, do I even want that? I don’t know. The loner vibe is comfortable, kind of. I mean, I love the silence sometimes. Just me, my thoughts, maybe some music playing in the background while I wander through ideas that are so much more interesting than whatever's going on in that crowded room. But then there’s that pesky little voice in my head that’s like, “Are you missing out?” That’s the neurotic bit, I think, always questioning everything, like if I made the right choice staying home or if I should’ve jumped at the chance to socialize. But why does it have to be either or? I can enjoy a good book while still wanting to connect with others. I just wish it didn’t feel like a performance every time. And honestly, I get annoyed with people pretty easily. Like, some of my classmates are just so… predictable. They write those essays that follow the rubric to a T, and I’m over here like, “But what if we turn it upside down? What if we challenge the norm?” I guess that’s the openness part. I crave the unusual, the out-of-the-box stuff that makes you rethink everything. But they just roll their eyes. They don’t understand. They want to follow and fit in. I don’t get it. Why follow a path that’s already paved when there are so many uncharted territories? But oh god, then there’s the anxiety that comes with it. The pressure to produce something brilliant, something that stands out from the rest. It’s exhausting! So I’ll start an essay and then just scrap it because it isn’t good enough—like I can’t find that sweet spot of creativity and structure at the same time. And honestly, who even cares how it’s structured? Does it matter if it flows perfectly with transitions or if it’s a jumble of thoughts that somehow make sense in my head? Speaking of which, I really can’t stand when people are hyper-organized. They have their schedules, their to-do lists all neatly checked off. I can barely commit to a morning routine. Some days I wake up thinking maybe today’s the day I’ll be productive, and then I end up watching cat videos or getting lost in some Wikipedia hole. Can’t even remember where I started half the time. So it’s weird, right? I’m surrounded by all these people who are so focused on their grades and careers, and I’m just here wondering if I should take up painting or start a podcast or both. And what happens if I fall short? If I don’t live up to whatever imaginary standard I’ve set for myself? But then again, whatever—maybe it’s just about the exploration, right? I crave experiences, new ideas, even if I feel like a ship lost at sea sometimes. At least it’s my sea, my storm, navigating through the chaos of what it means to be me. I think I’d prefer that over a calm but mundane lake any day. Hah, but then there’s the part of me that worries about the future, and how the hell I’m supposed to figure out what I want to do. The pressure feels crushing. Maybe I’ll find my way through the chaos—eventually—if I can just keep those anxious thoughts from spiral. Maybe that’s the key. Just keep swimming, keep searching, keep trying to dive deeper into the unknown.

Okay so I just remembered that party last weekend. Everyone was there, like literally everyone. I thrive in crowds but sometimes they exhaust me. That’s weird right? I get so pumped before I go but then halfway through I’m like where’s the exit? Too many people, too much noise, and my brain starts racing. I can’t even focus on one conversation. I bounce from group to group, and I love it, but I wish I could stay longer without feeling overwhelmed. I should have picked a couch spot instead of being in the middle of the dance floor. What was I thinking? I tried to talk to Jess about this project we’re working on for class but we got sidetracked talking about that reality show we both hate but keep watching anyway. It's a weird obsession, right? I enjoy the drama but it drives me crazy how scripted it all feels. Sometimes I worry if I talk too much about those shows, people will think I’m shallow. I mean, I could talk about deep stuff, but it’s exhausting. Like, politics? Ugh. That’s a hard pass. I’d rather chat about that wild pizza topping debate from last week—pineapple or anchovies, no one can decide. I mean, who even orders anchovies on pizza anymore? What were they thinking? But anyway, Jess gets it. We joke about random stuff, and I can’t help but admire how chill she is. I wish I could be more like her, you know? She seems to have it all together. I, on the other hand, am just over here freaking out about everything. Like, why am I still not done with that research paper? I started it weeks ago and it’s due tomorrow. Why can’t I just sit down and focus? I could’ve just banged it out in one sitting. Urgh. My brain is everywhere. I love my friends but sometimes I feel like I’m dragging them down with my chaotic energy. Like, do they even notice how I change the subject all the time? I just get so anxious about the current topic that I switch lanes. I don’t want to talk about things that make me nervous and spiral into a full-on panic, but I don’t want them to think I’m a flake either. It’s tough balancing what I want to say and what I think they want to hear. I’m always worried about being liked, even though I know they care. It’s exhausting. I’d rather just smile and laugh and pretend everything is fine. What’s the deal with those motivational quotes people post online? “Be the change you wish to see in the world!” like seriously? That sounds nice in theory, but it’s way more complicated than that. It’s almost like they don’t realize how complicated life can be. My friends share them all the time, and I want to support them but it makes me feel like I’m not doing enough. Why don’t I have it figured out by now? I’m twenty-one for crying out loud! But then I think, maybe it's okay not to have everything sorted out? I mean, everyone else seems to be faking it, right? I see them posting about their perfect lives, and it all looks so easy. Yet here I am stressing over a group project that feels more like a circus than a collaboration. I get along with everyone, we’re all friends, but the second we hit a bump in the road, it’s like the entire thing falls apart. It’s so frustrating! Maybe if I had just put my foot down to take charge, we wouldn’t be in this mess. But then I hate being the ‘bossy’ one, so I just end up going with the flow. Sometimes I daydream about just finding a quiet spot somewhere and reading non-stop for a few weeks. I never do, but it’s a nice thought. Just me, a stack of books, some snacks, and no one around to distract me. That would be bliss. Except, I also love hanging out with people too much to actually go through with it. Ahhhh! But that anxiety always pushes me toward socializing more. It’s a vicious cycle. I need to talk to someone about this, like a therapist or something. I can’t keep going like this. But then I think, how do I even find the time? Between classes, hangouts, and trying to keep my grades above water, when would I fit that in? It’s hard to prioritize my mental health when I’m so focused on keeping everyone else happy. It always comes back to that, doesn’t it? What a tangled web we weave. There goes my anxiety again, creeping up and making everything feel impossible. But I guess I just have to figure it out one day at a time. And hopefully, find time for that boring paper that’s haunting me.

I lost my favorite pen today. Not just any pen, but that one with the soft grip and the perfect flow. It’s so frustrating when you lose something you really like. I'm sure I had it in my bag when I was leaving class, but then I checked again and it’s not there. How do things just disappear? It’s driving me nuts. I mean, I could’ve lent it to someone but I don’t remember doing that. Wait, did I let Sarah borrow it during the lecture? I should text her. Ugh, I hate being anxious about things like this. I want to be chill and carefree, but I can't help it. My mind races. I wonder if she even remembers. She might think it’s just a pen but to me, it’s a whole mood. It helps me concentrate when I write. Writing helps me process all these thoughts. But what if she lost it? I can’t let this pen drama ruin my day. I could just go buy another one, but no, I prefer that one. Stupid attachment. Another thing I can be obsessive about. I always try to stay organized. I have my planner with schedules, sticky notes everywhere, color coding. The whole works. I love checking things off. Feels productive, feels good. But I also worry I might miss something important. Did I remember to email Professor Miller about that assignment? Better do that now. Control, control, control. God, I really hope she didn’t lose my pen. I should’ve said “no, you can’t use it” but it’s hard for me to say no. I want to help. I feel so bad if someone asks for a favor and I just go “no.” That feels terrible. I guess it’s because I care about my friends and people in general. Like today in class, when I noticed Tim looked a bit sad, I asked him if he was alright. He said he was just tired, but I could see it in his eyes. Sometimes I feel like I can read people really well, pick up on their emotions, even if they don’t say much. I like that about myself. I hope it helps them. What’s more, I often think I need to be there for everyone. It’s exhausting sometimes. I love being around people, but I worry if I’ll please them. Like when we went out for pizza last weekend, I kept asking if everyone liked the place, too much probably. It’s just how I am, I guess. I like seeing everyone happy, and I don’t want to ruin the vibe. But it makes me run around in circles. It’s like a tightrope act. Balancing my needs and their needs. I feel like I have to keep it all together. So much pressure. I can’t stand feeling overwhelmed, even if it’s just a little bit. I need my routine. It calms that storm inside my head. I’ll wake up, do my morning workout, eat breakfast, and then it’s off to classes. Always classes. I don’t mind them, they keep me busy, but I really wish I had more time to unwind. I dread weekends sometimes because I don’t want to miss out, but I also need that time to recharge. It’s like this internal battle. The thought of missing class turns my stomach. What if I miss something crucial? Plus, the profs don’t take kindly to absences. My grades matter. They need to stay high. I can’t jeopardize that. It’s not just about doing well; it’s about the responsibility I feel to succeed. My family expects it from me. They think I’m gonna be someone. I can’t let them down. What if I ever did? Oh God, the thought makes me cringe. Should I really text Sarah? It might make me feel better. And if she has my pen, it’ll calm me down. Maybe it’s just in the bottom of my bag. I should check one more time. But if she doesn’t have it, I’ll spiral again. Why do I do this? I wish I could be the type who just goes with the flow. If only I wasn’t so wound up. I’m always thinking ahead, planning out every little detail. Like what’s next week’s schedule? Or what’s on my to-do list tomorrow? I think I need to chill, but how? Anyway, I need to wrap this up. I can feel my heart racing thinking about all the stuff I have to do. I should probably take a deep breath. Focus on the little things. I’ll text Sarah. Maybe she’ll respond quickly. Maybe I can just relax for a tiny moment. Just let today unfold. Just once. But then I’ll remember the assignments, the readings, and all those things that pile up. It never ends.

I keep thinking about that sandwich I had last week. It was just a turkey sandwich, nothing fancy but the bread was fresh. I don’t really get excited about food but that one was good. Took it to go from that little deli on the corner. They always have the same stuff, predictable. I like that really. What's the point of all that variety? You end up with a plate full of choices and you can't figure out what you want. Give me a basic turkey sandwich any day. Why complicate things? Sometimes I just want to sit in silence and eat. I hate small talk, all the idle chatter just to fill the gaps. Last time I was at the deli, the guy behind the counter started asking me about my plans for the weekend. Like, why does he need to know? Just give me my food and let's keep it moving. I can’t believe how some people are so friendly, all smiles and asking questions like it’s a requirement. It’s exhausting. I don’t want to get into someone’s life story when I’m just there to pick up a sandwich. There's still this thing sitting on my desk that I need to return. I bought it online because I thought, why not try it? Turned out to be a waste. It’s just over there collecting dust, all those extra returns can be such a hassle. I don’t know why I thought it would be a good idea. People always talk about experiences but sometimes all I want is to not engage with anything that requires effort. I think boredom has its place in life too. Like, I’d rather be bored than busy. Busy feels like pressure and I’m not into that. I can’t stand deadlines looming over me. I don’t even get why people want to live their lives hustling all the time. Another semester is starting soon, and I can’t even muster up the energy to think about classes. It’s always the same. You go in, sit through lectures, take notes, and hope for the best on exams. Some people find it exciting. I just find it draining. My friends are all hyped up about it, the new books, the new professors, but I can’t say the same. I know what to expect, so why pretend I’m excited? Last semester, I managed to get through with minimal effort. I mean, just enough to scrape by and I feel like that’s all I really want. Why push myself for a grade that doesn’t change my life in the end? I don't seek validation through grades. I just want to finish and move on. Whenever we have group projects, I sit back and let others take the lead. It’s easier that way. People care too much about collaboration. You’d think they all want a medal for teamwork. I just roll my eyes because all I want is to do my part and be done. If I could go through life without having to deal with teams or partnerships, that’d be fantastic. Everyone is so obsessed with connecting and networking, but I don’t think it matters in the long run. I’m not going to need them and they’re not going to need me. It’s pointless. Let’s all just keep to ourselves and get our stuff done. I can’t even remember the last movie I saw. I usually just watch the same shows over and over. Comforting, I guess. It’s easier than keeping up with the latest trends or what everyone's raving about. I find myself scrolling mindlessly through streaming services, contemplating what to watch next but never really feeling compelled to commit to anything new. It’s just noise. Sometimes I think about what people talk about, like their hobbies and what they’re passionate about, but honestly, it just feels like a waste of time. I mean, who cares? There’s a time and place for everything. Honestly, I don’t get how people can spend hours online, always looking for something to share or something to say. Social media is nothing but chaos. I can’t handle it. My feed is cluttered with people’s constant updates, like what they had for breakfast or their latest workout. I’d rather hear crickets than engage in that. But I know that for many, it’s a release, a way to connect. Me, I’d rather sit in silence than have to sift through all that noise. I guess I just like my space. It’s approaching 20 minutes and I could probably keep going forever but there’s this weird satisfaction in just stopping. I don’t feel the pressure to keep talking, to keep thinking. Just let it all be. The sandwich, the group projects, the mundane. I guess that's it. Just straightforward and as uncomplicated as possible.

That shirt over there is really bright, I wonder if it’s new, it totally stands out, like something from a summer festival or one of those quirky thrift shops that are popping up everywhere with all the vintage stuff. Good vibes only, right? Speaking of good vibes, I was thinking about last weekend’s party at Jake’s place, everyone was there, and the music was perfect! I love how people come together over music, it’s like an instant connection. You throw in some dancing, and it’s as if all the worries just float away, right? I think I met like five new people that night, their names are already blending together, but their energy was contagious. Okay. Focus. What was I saying? Oh! Party vibes. I was chatting with this girl, Melissa, and she had the funniest stories about traveling. She’s been everywhere—backpacking through Europe, weekend trips to the mountains. I have to ask her what her favorite spot is because I want to travel more. There’s something so liberating about exploring new places, meeting new faces. Even just the thought of a train trip to the beach makes me smile. The feeling of the ocean breeze, the sound of waves, and maybe a fresh coconut drink. I should plan a beach trip soon; everyone loves the beach. A little sun doesn’t hurt. But back to that party, I can still hear the music in my head. Whoever picked the playlist deserves a medal. Totally set the mood. And, oh! The snacks! I really appreciate when people bring food to parties, it's like a love language. Chips and dips are classic, but someone made these mini empanadas. Like, wow, so good. I ended up talking to the person who made them. I think his name was Sam? He was so passionate about cooking. We talked about recipes for an hour! I swear, if you can bond over food, you can bond over anything. Did I even get his social media? I hope so! I have to stay in touch with cool people like him. And then there was this hilarious game of charades. I was so into it! I love games like that because everybody just lets loose. I can’t even remember half the things we acted out, but I know I was a total goofball. I ended up imitating a squirrel, and everyone laughed so hard, it made my night. Being silly is just freeing, you know? I think some people take life too seriously, and it’s like—come on, we’re here to have fun! But some people hold back, and I get it; everyone has their stuff. Oh, speaking of serious stuff, I’ve been thinking more about my classes lately. I really enjoy my psychology course, especially the part about human behavior. It’s fascinating how we all have different motivations and personalities, and how our environments shape us. I like learning about theories and then relating them to real-life situations. Human connections, friendships, relationships—they’re all so intricate. You can see how high energy and extroversion can positively affect a group dynamic. I wonder if I should do a project on social interactions. That could be cool, right? Maybe I could gather data about how outgoing people influence group activities compared to more reserved individuals. Ugh, group work. It can be frustrating at times when you have to deal with all those different personalities. Like, some people just want to coast through it, and I’m like, “Come on, let’s create something awesome together!” It’s not about just finishing the task; it’s about collaboration and discovering new ideas together. Sometimes I just wish everyone had the same excitement as I do. But I think that’s what I love about studying psychology—understanding differences. I also find it helpful in my daily interactions. Like when someone seems off, asking them if they’re okay instead of just assuming they’re fine. It’s amazing how a simple check-in can make someone feel valued. I can’t wait for the next social event. I just want to meet more people! There’s something exhilarating about connecting with strangers, sharing a laugh, and creating memories. Ah! I really need to find out what’s happening this weekend. There’s always something happening around campus—game nights, movie screenings, open mic nights, even art exhibits. I should keep some flyers handy. I mean, artsy stuff is so cool! I love how people express themselves through different mediums. I want to try painting again. I haven’t painted since high school, and I remember just loving how it felt—such an outlet for creativity. Okay, I really need to wrap this up soon. When was the last time I went hiking? I should definitely make time for that, too. The fresh air and the views, plus it’s a great way to just disconnect and recharge. Nature always brings me back to life. There’s this trail I love, and I could bring a friend or two. Or just go solo and enjoy the silence. Either way, it’s going to be epic. I’m feeling that good energy building up just thinking about it!

okay so I was thinking about how I can’t stand small talk like seriously why does everyone want to just chat about the weather or what brand of coffee is better than another coffee it’s like there are bigger issues to worry about I mean I want to dive deep into things like what do you think about the meaning of life or if aliens exist and why nobody’s talking about that seriously I crave those conversations but then again when you’re in a group of people it’s like you have to play this game where everyone pretends everything’s fine and dandy but it’s never that simple and that’s exhausting sometimes I just want to shout at people and say let’s be real for once who cares about the latest Netflix show just tell me your biggest fear or your wildest dream and let’s talk about that but no they want to keep it surface level and I get it in a way it’s like we’re all just trying to cope with whatever mess is happening in our lives but I crave authenticity and sometimes I worry that I push people away because I’m too intense or maybe it’s just that they don’t get me and how can they? I don’t even get myself sometimes why do I care so much about what other people think and then I’m jumping into another thought like how I can’t handle criticism at all like I want to be the life of the party and then someone says something that rubs me the wrong way and it’s like a switch flips and I’m spiraling down this rabbit hole of self-doubt and insecurity wow isn’t that fun not really but I guess that’s life right? I remember telling a friend once that I feel like I’m always on stage like I need to be entertaining or engaging to people or they’ll just drift away and I hate the drifting away part it makes me feel so anxious like I should be doing something to keep them engaged but at the same time I also want to be the one who doesn’t give a crap about anything and just lives life on my own terms but it’s this constant push and pull like a tug of war in my brain and I’m exhausted thinking about it. Can I just be someone who sits back and observes these social interactions because I find them so fascinating and ridiculous at the same time but then I get bored if I don’t join in and then I have to jump back into the fray and crack jokes or be that friend who always has a crazy story to share. Then there are the moments of clarity when I’m completely alone and I realize how much I love exploring new things like traveling and food and art but then my mind spirals back to how does this fit into a career which seems to be just about making money and what’s the point of that if I’m not happy and can I fit all this travel and discovery into my life without feeling burnt out because let’s be real I can’t handle that pressure all the time. I’m thinking about this trip I want to take next month and I can already feel the excitement building but then I worry about the logistics and making sure I have everything planned out and what if something goes wrong because it always does and do I really want to be dealing with all the stress when I just want to have a good time? But then again isn’t the adventure the point of it all? I love getting lost in new cities and trying new foods and meeting new people who have such different lives from mine but sometimes those moments get overshadowed by my fears like what if I can’t communicate or what if they think I’m weird but then I remind myself that being weird is what makes life interesting and it’s wild how you can make friends across cultures just by sharing a laugh or a smile but not everyone gets that and that’s when I start to feel frustrated it’s like I need to connect with everyone but not everyone is open to that kind of energy and then I’m left feeling drained and wondering if I should just stay home next time but I know I won’t because the world is too beautiful and full of possibility. I should probably learn to chill out though because I think I get too high-strung about everything sometimes and it’s not healthy like I can’t let my emotions dictate everything I do even though it feels like they’re in control way more than they should be. There’s this balance I have to find between being the outgoing, social butterfly and taking care of my mental health. And then I spiral back to thinking about how all of this relates to what I’m doing with my life right now, like am I making the right choices and why do I feel the pressure to even figure that out at my age? But then I notice it’s a cycle and I get stuck in it and suddenly my room feels like it’s closing in on me and I have to escape because sitting with these thoughts is too much sometimes. Socializing is a needed distraction but it’s a double-edged sword and I both love it and hate it and I don’t know how to find that middle ground. Maybe I just need to embrace the chaos of my thought process and let it ride.

So, I’m thinking about that time I overslept and missed my morning class—again. I really should have gone to bed earlier but I got lost in that rabbit hole of articles about space exploration and how they found water on Mars. The way they talk about it, it's almost poetic. Water on Mars could mean life. Or did mean life. It makes me feel like a tiny speck in the universe, like my worries don’t even matter, those existential thoughts swirl in my head, and I can’t shake them off. I get so caught up in thinking about that stuff. It’s fascinating but also anxiety-inducing. I mean, what if we’re not alone? Or worse, what if we’re the only ones? I feel like I need to do something about it. But then again, what can I do? My thoughts get tangled up in themselves, like the cords of my headphones that I can never seem to untangle. I should really clean my room. It’s a mess. I have clothes everywhere, books stacked high, and food wrappers that I keep saying I’ll throw away but never do. It’s disgusting. I know what a clean space can do for my brain but I just can’t seem to find the motivation. Or maybe I don’t want to face the reality of it all, the chaos around me reflecting the chaos in my mind. And it makes me think of how I interact with people. I don’t really like social situations. I mean, I can do them—like the other day at that group project meeting, I didn’t speak up much. I just like to observe. It’s easier that way. People can be exhausting. The way they talk over each other, it’s like a competition to see who can be the loudest. I end up zoning out, focusing on the little things like the way someone’s shoe squeaks when they shift their weight or how I wish the room was more organized. Oh god, the thought of it all just makes me want to crawl under my blanket and disappear. I’ve been reading about personality traits lately, trying to figure myself out or maybe just make sense of all this. I mean, they say I’m low on extraversion but high on neuroticism. That makes sense. I can’t help but worry about everything. Did I say the wrong thing in class? Did I write enough for that assignment? Maybe I should have reached out to that friend who seems distant, but what if she doesn’t want to hear from me? I overthink everything until I just end up doing nothing. It’s so paralyzing. It’s like I want to connect but the thought of it sends me spiraling into doubts. And then there’s the conscientiousness part. I’m pretty good at keeping track of my responsibilities, but it’s also really draining. I have this planner that I color-code with tasks and deadlines, but sometimes just looking at it makes my stomach turn. I can’t help but feel overwhelmed. I admire those who can go with the flow. I wish I could be more like that. Why can’t I just let things be? Why must I structure everything? I crave that freedom yet I hold myself back. I really don’t understand people who are easygoing. My friend was telling me about this party she went to. She said it was just a bunch of people hanging out, laughing, and having fun. That sounds exhausting. I mean, if I were there, I’d be hyper-aware of how I’m coming across, analyzing every laugh, every eye roll. I wouldn’t be able to relax. I’d be too busy trying to assess the dynamics, figuring out who likes who, what to say to not seem awkward. I’m way too serious about everything. It isn’t that I don’t enjoy life. I just... experience it differently. Sometimes, I think I have too many feelings. Is that even a thing? I feel things deeply—joy, sadness, frustration. It all hits me like a wave. And openness, yeah, I guess that’s true too. I’m always looking for new experiences. I want to travel, see new places, meet different people, but then the anxiety creeps in. What if something goes wrong? What if I can’t handle it? What if I’m stuck somewhere far away, not knowing anyone? It’s this constant push and pull between wanting to leap and fearing the heights. It’s all a lot to process. I think I need to get it out of my system, sort through all these tangled thoughts. But maybe that’s just how it is. Just swirling thoughts that never settle. And, oh, I should probably finish that assignment before it’s too late. I always leave it to the last minute—it’s a habit. A stress habit. I’ll tackle it before I drown in worry. Or maybe I’ll just pull out my headphones, hit play on something comforting, and try to float for a little while longer.

I can’t believe it’s already getting late. Time flies when I’m just thinking about all the things I need to do, like finish that paper that’s due next week and I still haven’t really started it. Why do I always leave things until the last minute? Is it because I want it to be perfect? I mean, obviously, it’s like I can’t help but dig deeper, I’m constantly researching, and I don’t know if it’s because I’m curious or just anxious about not knowing enough. I can’t stand the idea of being unprepared. There’s so much pressure coming from everywhere, my family expects me to do well, my professors are not exactly subtle with their grading, and let’s not even talk about how competitive it is. Everyone seems so confident, and I’m over here stressing about every little detail, worried that I’ll miss something critical. Then there’s social media buzzing with everyone’s highlights. It’s exhausting. Scrolling through Instagram, seeing everyone having fun, going out, making connections, living their best lives while I’m just here contemplating whether I should send a quick text or just avoid it altogether because what if they don’t respond? What if I say something dumb? Honestly, even responding to group messages is like navigating a maze for me. I can’t shake off the feeling that everyone is judging what I say, analyzing every word, every typo. It’s like I operate on a different wavelength. I wish I were more inclined to just go out and enjoy the scene but the thought of mingling with strangers is terrifying. And then there’s the thought of my future. It’s like a looming cloud that never goes away. What am I even doing with my life? Am I on the right path? I feel like everyone has their life figured out but me. I get so wrapped up in these spirals of self-doubt, it’s a bit smothering. I overanalyze every decision, every class I choose to enroll in. There’s a part of me that craves structure and stability but there’s also this insatiable desire for exploration. Why does it have to be so complicated? I want to see the world, learn new things, meet fascinating people, but stepping out of my comfort zone feels like standing on the edge of a cliff, teetering and unsure whether to jump or back away. Sometimes I think about my friends, the few I have. They seem carefree, yet supportive but can they really understand what’s going on inside my head? I doubt it. I mean, they can try but how can you explain the feeling of dread when you sit down to write? It’s awful. Every time I open a blank page, the words seem to vanish. I wish I could just let it flow like they do. I get so self-critical that it’s like I’m my own worst enemy, holding up a mirror and reflecting every flaw back to me. I find it fascinating how people can connect so easily while I feel like I’m pushing against an invisible wall. Am I too rigid? Too focused on what’s right rather than what feels right? Is struggling with neuroticism part of the deal? It’s like I’m constantly on edge, a bundle of nerves that can’t relax. I keep thinking if I just buckle down and get through all of this, it’ll pay off in the end. I mean, maybe success could be my antidote to all this anxiety. But success is just as scary, isn’t it? What if I succeed and then I have to sustain it? The pressure would only become greater. Okay, and then there are these moments when I feel completely out of my depth. Like in class discussions, everyone is so eloquent, throwing out ideas and theories as if they’ve absorbed the knowledge through sheer osmosis. I try to contribute but my voice feels small, like it’s drowned out by the louder, more persuasive arguments. I know I have ideas, interesting perspectives, but they never come out as clearly as I want. It’s like my mind is racing while my mouth is stuck, fumbling over words that refuse to cooperate. And then there are the late-night thoughts. They’re the worst. Lying in bed, replaying the day, dissecting every interaction, wishing I could redo conversations, fix mistakes, and finally get it right for once. That’s when all the worries flood in. Am I enough? Will I ever fit in? I have this longing to explore ideas, dive deeper into subjects that fascinate me but it’s never without that persistent voice in my head saying I’m not cut out for this. It’s relentless. But then I catch myself dreaming of what could be, traveling to places where nobody knows me, experiencing new cultures. Each trip would be a fresh start, a chance to reinvent myself. I want to capture the beauty of the world, to experience it fully and be part of the grand story. This openness I have sometimes collides with my anxiety and it’s like a tug-of-war inside my mind. It’s frustrating but also kind of exciting. What if I could harness both? What if I could find a way to channel my worries into something bigger, something meaningful? That would be fantastic. But for now, I guess I’ll just keep typing until I sort through the chaos a bit more.

Why is my phone buzzing again? Oh, probably another group chat thing I can’t even keep up with. Group projects should be banned. I mean seriously, it’s like everyone has a different idea of what the assignment is and I just want to sit in my room and do my own thing but instead I'm stuck in this back-and-forth where I need to babysit the entire team like it's my job. Everyone thinks they can just throw their ideas in and I’m left to pick up the pieces. It’s exhausting. Why do I even do this? I could just say no, but then I feel bad. I hate that. That feeling of guilt sitting there in the back of my mind like a heavy weight. Speaking of weight, I really need to start working out again. I say that every week. It’s like, okay maybe I’ll go for a run today. But then I just don’t. The thought of running sounds exhausting. I think about all those people at the gym, what are they thinking looking at me? I could just be judging myself but I swear I can feel their eyes on me. This is all so stupid. I don't even know why I care what they think. My roommate said something about going to this party this weekend. Hard pass. I can’t deal with small talk. I can barely handle real talk, much less pretending to like people. Just shows how little I fit in. I mean, do I even want to go? No, not really. But what if everyone talks about it on Monday? Ugh, that FOMO thing is so annoying, but it’s whatever. I’ll just scroll through Instagram and see everyone’s photos later. That’s enough socializing, right? I keep getting these emails for events on campus and I think, like, who even goes to this stuff? I can’t picture myself standing around pretending to laugh at jokes that aren't funny. It’s like, what’s the point? Social interaction just sounds draining. Sometimes I feel like just closing the curtains and shutting out the world completely. I think I do that a lot. Just want to burrow into my blankets and disappear. But there’s always that looming feeling. I know I need to do something productive. Maybe I’ll finally read those books piled up on my desk. Although it’s been weeks and I still haven’t even cracked one open. And what about my classes? I’m behind on so much. I think about it constantly but when it comes time to actually sit down and study, I get distracted by every little thing. The way the light hits the wall, the texture of my blanket, my phone buzzing again. Just look at it! I can’t ignore it. Someone else probably needs info on the group project I don't care about. Do I even want to know what they said? Probably not. But I’ll look anyway, get annoyed for no reason, and then decide to just keep ignoring everything. And then I think about money. I’m always broke. Between tuition and rent and food, I can’t even keep track. My bank account looks like a horror movie, every time I look it feels like a jump scare. I feel guilty that my parents help me out sometimes but they really shouldn’t have to. I should have been more responsible but here I am, living on instant ramen and tears. I guess I could go find a part-time job but thinking about it makes me more anxious than I already am. I don’t think I could handle another thing on my plate. There’s a pile of laundry just sitting there. I have clean clothes somewhere in that mess, I'm sure of it. I just don’t want to deal with it. I keep telling myself I’ll do it tomorrow and tomorrow stretches on into infinity. I wonder if people would judge me for living in such chaos. I don’t care, though, because it’s my chaos and it’s comfortable in a way. Even if I know it’s not productive. I should probably figure out what I want to do with my life, too, right? Everyone else seems to have it all figured out and I just float through, day-to-day. Maybe I could have a plan. But then again, that sounds like too much effort. I mean, being organized seems like a joke when I can’t even get a handle on my own thoughts. What’s the time? I should probably wrap this up. Or not? Maybe I’ll keep going just to see if something magical happens. Probably won’t, though. Just me and my swirling thoughts endlessly bouncing around. I can’t even keep track anymore; it’s like trying to catch water with a sieve. I should just embrace the chaos as it is. It’s my chaos.

Okay so I just remembered that time last summer when I went to that outdoor music festival with my friends and it was just pure chaos but in the best way possible. All these bands I didn’t know just playing and we danced like total idiots, I can't believe how good it felt to be surrounded by people, strangers even, and just vibing to the same beat. It’s like everyone’s on the same wavelength and I thrive in those environments. Love it. I mean, it’s funny how I can just meet someone in line for tacos and suddenly we’re talking about our favorite albums, it’s just so easy sometimes. Anyway, tacos. I could go for some right now. I wonder if that place around the corner is still open. Their fish tacos are incredible, like melt-in-your-mouth goodness. I should really go back there. I always get the same thing though, gotta mix it up. Maybe I’ll try a burrito next time? But I’m not really a burrito person. It’s cumbersome you know, just get messy and I hate being sticky. Sticky fingers at a music festival? Totally not. But oh man, the way the sun sets behind the stage, golden hour catching all the faces of the crowd, everyone just soaking it all in. It’s like I can’t help but smile because that’s human connection right there. Thinking of that reminds me of how I sometimes catch myself just observing things. Like in class, I’ll be sitting there, listening to the professor go on about Shakespeare or whatever, and I’ll find myself staring at someone’s shoelaces or that weird poster on the wall. It’s like I totally zone out but not because I’m bored, but just because I’m taking everything in, processing who’s in the room, how they’re interacting. I guess that aligns with my openness, you know? I just want to explore everything, people, ideas, experiences. It’s probably why I have a million hobbies that I jump between, the latest being pottery which is both relaxing and frustrating. Pottery is great until you realize your hands are caked with clay and you’ve got five failed bowls on the shelf. So, like, I’ll text my friends about it but then they ask about coming over and honestly I just want to keep it as my thing, no pressure from anyone else. I enjoy sharing experiences but also love keeping parts of my life private. That balance is nice, right? I mean, I'd probably make a terrible therapist because I wouldn’t want to deal with all that emotional stuff, and where’s the fun in that? I’d rather have a fun night out or a spontaneous road trip than sit in a room and talk about feelings, which I guess is why I don’t have a million close friends, just a solid group of really good ones. Speaking of road trips, I’ve got this urge to hit the open road. No plan, just me and my playlist blasting, discovering little towns or maybe going to the coast. Just thinking about that salty air makes me feel alive. I can’t even remember the last trip I took, was it last winter? Or was it just back-to-back weekends filled with spontaneous adventures? Sometimes I think life is too short to sit still and I’d rather be out doing something than staring at my screen. Like even right now, I could be doing anything. Should I text someone? Get a group together? I could go to that art exhibit opening this weekend, but I kind of want to just do it alone, you know, breathe in the art, contemplate it instead of chatting about it. Oh, and what about my classes this semester? I’m definitely taking that creative writing class because I need a break from all this data analysis. Those professors keep talking about learning outcomes and assessment criteria but do they even understand how to engage with students? I mean half the time they drone on like we’re not all itching to just jump out of our seats. What happened to engaging discussions? I’d rather have debates than passive lectures. But that’s the thing, I love challenging ideas, and I often find myself just throwing my hand up with thoughts that come to me, but I know it can rub some people the wrong way. I probably step on toes sometimes but not out of malice, just out of excitement to share my perspective. Oh gosh, I just remembered I need to grab that book from the library, the one on creativity and innovation. Should get on that… but maybe after I grab those tacos. Gosh, can’t stop thinking about food now. Maybe some ice cream too? Ahh, the freedom of choices! Is it weird that I think about food so much? It’s like fuel for all my wild ideas. I can already feel the day unfolding. So many possibilities. What’s next?

So I was just thinking about how people can be so exhausting. Like, why do they need to talk all the time, I mean does it really matter what they think about the weather or whatever? I’m not even sure why I’m here today. It's just that nagging feeling that I should be doing something, but I can't figure out what that is. I have so many thoughts but also no thoughts. That's what it feels like sometimes, just a jumble of noise in my head and I can’t quite pin down anything concrete. And then there’s the whole thing with social media. Everyone loves to post their lives online. Why? It's just a highlight reel and I can't decide if it's depressing or amusing or both. I feel detached, looking in, like I’m watching a really bad reality show. I guess it’s ironic because I’m on it too sometimes, just scrolling without really connecting or engaging. Then there’s anxiety. It’s like the default setting for my brain. I mean, I’ll just be sitting there and then suddenly I spiral into thoughts of, I don’t know, what if I missed a deadline or what if someone hates me? It doesn't help that I have this constant nagging feeling that I’m not doing enough. Last week I did nothing. Just sat in my room, too tired to even make a meal. I think about making dinner, but then I think about the dishes afterward, and it becomes this huge obstacle. So I just eat cereal for dinner again. But even then, I overthink it. Should I have almond milk or regular? Do I really want cereal? It's a simple choice, but it feels so loaded. I don't get people who can just breeze through life and be okay with everything. I mean, how? I mean, school doesn’t help with that. You get all this pressure, grades, expectations, professors acting like it’s all just an afterthought. And then I’m sitting there in class, trying to focus on the lecture, but my mind is so far away thinking about how I should probably be studying for that exam next week but who am I kidding because I won’t do it anyway. I’ll just wing it and hope for the best. That always works out, right? But then if it doesn't, I’ll just be back in that cycle of self-doubt and regret. I don’t know how to be more organized or productive. I’ve tried lists, planners, color-coding. But I never follow through. I buy all the fancy pens, but it just ends up being this thing that sits on my desk collecting dust. And that’s fine, I guess, because I don’t really care about that either. Should I care? Sometimes I think I should care, but the effort seems too much. I see other people hustling and grinding, and I feel like there’s something wrong with me for not being like that. I should be more motivated. Maybe I’m just lazy? But it’s more than that. It’s like I’m trapped in my own head, and it’s suffocating. I don’t want to hang out with people. It’s complicated. Like, I don’t want to be rude or hurt anyone’s feelings, but the idea of socializing makes me want to crawl into a hole. I’d rather just watch something mindless on TV or read a book, but then I can't even focus on that sometimes either because my mind wanders, and I’ll start analyzing the characters and their motivations instead of just enjoying the plot. It’s a mess. I guess it’s all just part of being me. And I think about the future. What if I end up like this forever? Just a hermit living in my room, eating cereal and scrolling through social media, watching life happen to everyone else. I don’t even know if I want to change, honestly. Change seems like so much work. Am I supposed to want to be different? I just feel like I can’t breathe sometimes. It’s all connected, I know. My low extraversion, high neuroticism, and all that. It feels like a trap I can’t get out of, and I should probably talk to someone about it but then I start overthinking that too. Who would even get it? What if they just think I’m being dramatic? So I don’t. I just keep it all in, swirling in my head until it’s too much, and then it goes quiet again. I sit and stare out the window wondering if I should really care about anything at all. I don’t know. It’s such a mess.

The clock says 3:17 PM and I still haven’t gotten my laundry done. It’s in the corner of my room, the basket overflowing and staring at me like it’s judging my life choices. I should really just do it. But then again, it’s so daunting, all those clothes mixed together. What if I ruin my favorite sweater? That one I bought ages ago and can’t seem to replace because it’s the only one I really like. Why do I even have so many clothes that I don’t wear? It’s like they just sit there, a little army of regret. Maybe I should just get rid of the stuff I never wear. But that feels like a big decision. I could always put it off until tomorrow. Tomorrow is when I’ll finally feel motivated. Or at least that’s what I tell myself. I remember the last time I tried to clean my room. It turned into this huge mess. I started organizing and then realized there were so many things I didn’t know what to do with. Old papers, broken pencils, that weird trinket my aunt gave me for my birthday. What do you even do with a trinket? I mean, I appreciate the thought but it’s just clutter. I can’t throw it away because it feels rude but it’s just sitting there and I don’t display it. It’s funny how people have different meanings for objects. I wish I could just not think about it. If I could just make it disappear. But I can’t, I can’t get rid of it. Maybe someday I’ll just have a massive clean-out day and toss it all. But, ugh, that day never comes. Speaking of clutter, my phone is such a mess too. I can’t even remember the last time I cleared my apps. Notifications everywhere, red dots taunting me. I wonder if I have messages from friends I haven’t seen in ages. The thought of answering feels like the world’s largest weight on my shoulders. I really want to see them, but is it too much to reach out? What if they don’t want to hang out? What if they’re busy and I’m just a pile of insecurities crashing into their lives? I should probably get over it. Just text, just invite them out. But it’s always the same cycle. Overthinking, second-guessing, and then doing nothing. It’s easier to stay put. I find it funny how other people can just chat away, slip into social situations like it’s a warm bath. Meanwhile, I’m over here calculating every possible outcome, every shift in expression. I can picture them all hanging out and laughing while I’m stuck in my head thinking of a million scenarios. What if they think I’m weird? What if I guy I like is there and I get tongue-tied? Just meet up already. But then I think about how awkward it could get, the pauses for words, the pressure to fill the silence. Ugh, I just need a break from being so anxious all the time. Then there’s the layer of feeling bad about it, like I’m letting my friends down. They probably don’t see it that way. But I see their notifications lighting up and feel like I’m missing out. Everything blending into a cocktail of social anxiety and feeling left behind. I just wish they’d reach out to me, just send a text that says “hey, we miss you” you know? Just a nudge to get me out there. But I know that’s selfish. They have their lives too. My mind starts racing again, thinking about the future. What’s going to happen after school? Will I even find a job? Do I have what it takes to survive in the real world? I think about how I’m not really organized. My résumé looks like it was written by a toddler. What if no one wants to hire me? I barely even keep track of deadlines. I hope I can find something that won’t make me stressed out, something peaceful. But the word “peaceful” feels so far away. What if I just disappeared for a while? That sounds appealing. Just take a break from people, from responsibilities. But then I think about how lonely that would be—being alone with my thoughts. I wouldn’t last long. I’m not an island, I can’t do this alone. Maybe I should just commit to one small thing today. Like, actually start that laundry. Just follow through and find something to change. But there’s that voice again, saying it’s too much. Just one step, I think. One thing at a time. But then I get paralyzed by the weight of it all.
