I love that sense of camaraderie when we’re all up and cheering for each other. It makes the whole thing more exciting. The laughter, the friendly banter, it’s just the best. Speaking of laughter, I really appreciate how I can just talk to anyone. Like I was in line at the grocery store the other day and just started chatting with the person behind me about how the best snacks are always on the top shelves and seriously how ridiculous it is that we have to use tippy toes for the good stuff. She laughed and we ended up sharing snack recommendations. Cinnamon sugar pita chips are life-changing, by the way! I think people appreciate that kind of connection – I know I do. Also, I’m really into planning things out, like if there’s an event coming up, I’ll want to make sure everything is organized. I love making checklists. There’s something satisfying about ticking things off. Makes me feel accomplished. I’ve got some friends who are all, “Let’s just wing it!” And I’m like, “No way!” I can’t deal with that! It’s way more comforting to have things laid out. Like my study group for finals, we’ve got a whole schedule mapped out. We meet every Saturday morning and it’s always fun to sit in a circle–I mean, who doesn’t love a whiteboard and colorful markers? Discussions bouncing around, ideas flying everywhere. I just feel more secure knowing I have a plan. But then sometimes I wonder if I’m too rigid with my plans. Is it good to be spontaneous? My roommate is the exact opposite, she flies by the seat of her pants, and although it’s kind of crazy, I can’t help but admire how she just goes with the flow. I feel like I’m constantly balancing this need for structure with the joy of spontaneity. It’s interesting how people are just different like that. Finding the balance is challenging but I guess it makes life more interesting. Got some friends who can also make plans on the fly, and it always turns into this wild adventure. I remember one time we decided on a road trip to the beach at the last minute; we just packed snacks and jumped in the car. I was nervous at first because I like everything in its place, but it turned out to be one of the best days ever. We blasted music and sang along, even when the songs were awful. It was freeing to just let go, even if it was a little outside my comfort zone. I mean, who knew getting lost could be so much fun? Then there’s social media. I can be scrolling for hours sometimes. I love seeing pictures from friends, it keeps me feeling connected, even if I can't see them every day. Instagram is full of smiles, and I just love when people share their happy moments. But like, when people share drama or negativity, I just want to scroll past. I really thrive on positivity and it’s tough when that gets interrupted by stress. And then there’s the whole idea of volunteering and helping others – I’m super into that. It’s like that feeling you get when you see someone smile because of something you did. I always sign up for local charity events and it feels fulfilling to be part of something bigger. I love getting my friends involved too. Like, we did this community clean-up recently, and although we were all exhausted afterward, we felt so good about contributing. I really think about how important it is to surround myself with people who care. Sometimes, I’m the one who plans everything, and everyone is totally on board, but if they aren’t, we’ll just figure something out. I guess that’s my strength: making sure everyone feels included and valued. It’s like this little superpower of mine. I don’t consider myself a natural leader, but when things need direction, I just step up because it feels right. I want everyone to have a good time and feel connected. It’s just that little spark of joy I look for, and honestly, I think that’s what life is all about—a mix of experiences, relationships, and good vibes.

Okay so I just waved at some random person walking past my window, they didn’t wave back. I wonder if they saw me or if they were just lost in thought. It’s kind of funny how you can be in a crowded place and still feel completely alone, but that’s not really me. I thrive on people. I love being around them. Sometimes I think, do I talk too much? But then I remember that silence is awkward. I would rather fill it with anything than just sit there with dead air. I often wonder what people really think when I talk. Is it entertaining? Annoying? I should probably just ask. But then what if they say annoying? That would suck. The other day, I went to that new café that just opened up in town. Super cute, all hipster vibes. The barista was this tattooed dude with piercings everywhere and he seemed super chill. I don’t even remember what I ordered; I think it was just whatever he suggested. You know how you can meet someone and instantly vibe with them? That’s how I felt. I ended up chatting with him for like twenty minutes while he made my drink. I don’t even know if he was supposed to be talking that much. But he didn’t seem to mind. I can usually tell when someone’s into a conversation or when they’re just trying to get the job done and go on with their day. I guess I just draw it out of people, huh? Also, there was this old couple sitting nearby who looked like they’ve been married forever. They were just sitting there quietly sipping their coffee together and every now and then, they’d just look at each other and grin. So cute. I wish I had something like that, but right now, I’m just all over the place, hopping from one person to another like I’m collecting stories. I think connection is the most valuable thing we have, right? Like I could sit and listen to someone talk about their life for hours, especially if they have a wild story. I love how everyone’s got their own story. Last week I was at this party, and some guy was sharing how he backpacked through South America for six months. I was glued to my drink trying to soak up every detail. There were so many places I’ve never even heard of, and that got me thinking—there’s a whole world out there. I should get out there and explore! But also I’m kind of lazy sometimes. I mean, it sounds super cool to travel all the time, but then there’s the hassle of airports and waiting lines and security checks. I can already feel the frustration rising just thinking about it. But then, if I think about all the amazing cultures and people I could meet, it might be worth it. Speaking of travel, I remember when I went to New York City last summer. What a rush. Everything was just so chaotic, like the streets were alive with energy. I swear I felt like I was on a movie set. I went to Times Square and took a million pictures because how could I not? All those bright lights and people hustling by—all kinds of people. I made a couple of friends at a bar there, and they took me to this secret rooftop spot that overlooked the skyline. It’s like every corner, there’s something happening, something new. I could live there, but not forever. I’d need to find a way to breathe sometimes, you know? Anyway, all these little interactions stick with me. Like the time I was at a bus stop and started chatting with an artist. He told me he drew murals all over the city. I could see the passion in his eyes as he talked, and I found myself wishing I could paint something beautiful like that. But I don’t know, my creativity feels more like a whirlwind of ideas than something solid. I guess that’s what makes it fun? It’s exciting to think outside the box. Follow where my brain goes. It’s all about those experiences and memories. But then again, I always have this little voice in my head telling me to focus. Finish what I started. My planner is filled with deadlines, things to do, places to go. I love checking things off. I used to feel like it limited me, but now it feels comforting. Structure keeps me grounded. Plus, I get to plan all these fun adventures. It balances out! And then there’s my friends. I have a good mix of personalities in my circle, which keeps things interesting. Some are quiet, but they let me rant, and they find the humor in my chaos. Others are just as loud and wild as I am. It’s fun to see how we all fit together. Just makes me realize that everyone has something important to say, even if I’m usually the one saying it the most.

Okay so I can’t believe I forgot to send that email. It’s driving me crazy thinking about the deadline creeping up and how I have to remember everything. I keep a checklist, but even that sometimes makes me anxious because what if I forget to check something off? I don’t think I’m good at juggling tasks. People are always saying multitasking is key but honestly, I can’t do it. I get too overwhelmed. Just focus on one thing at a time, Sarah. It’s just so hard sometimes when my mind jumps around from thought to thought. I remember my roommate always says I’m too much of a “people pleaser.” It’s like I can’t help it though. I want everyone to be happy. What if they don’t like me? What if I say something that annoys them? That keeps me up at night more than I’d like to admit. I mean, it’s good to be nice and all, but it feels like a burden more than a blessing sometimes. It’s the small things like when a friend asks to borrow my notes for class and I say yes but inside I’m worrying if they’ll return them. I want to be trustworthy, but it’s just nerve-wracking. I think I like routine. It feels safe. Wake up, get breakfast, go to class. But I can’t help but feel a little trapped. Some of my friends are going out this weekend and I really don’t want to go. The thought of a crowded bar makes my stomach churn. Everyone laughing and shouting and the music too loud. I hate feeling like I have to shout to talk to someone. And what if I don’t know anyone there? Then I’ll just be standing awkwardly in a corner hoping someone talks to me. And probably overthinking everything I say. Better to stay in and binge-watch my favorite series again. I’m really into that one drama lately. It’s intense and emotional, and I get so wrapped up in the characters’ lives that I forget my own worries for a bit. But then I think about how I should be socializing and meeting new people. Everyone does it. I wonder if I’m just being anti-social or if it’s okay to not want to go out all the time. I should probably talk to someone about it… but talking about feelings makes me feel so exposed. I guess that’s just part of being me. I mean there are days I feel like I can’t get out of my head. The constant racing thoughts, the “what ifs” looping endlessly. I could write a book about all the things that worry me. Like, what if I don’t get a job after graduating? Or what if I mess up on my next exam? Or what if I say the wrong thing and someone gets upset? I keep everything so organized. My desk is neat, everything has a place, but sometimes it all feels like it could fall apart in an instant. I think about my mom and how she always told me to take deep breaths when I’m feeling overwhelmed. Breathe in, breathe out, and count to five. Sometimes it helps, but sometimes it doesn’t and I just spiral. I like helping people, though. If a friend is stressed about an assignment, I jump in to offer support. I want to tell them everything will be okay. Maybe if I help them feel better, I’ll feel better too. It makes me feel worthwhile, I guess. But then there’s the fine line of overextending myself, where I end up drained and exhausted. I think I have learned to set some boundaries, but it’s hard. I don’t really believe in going with the flow. What if it leads to chaos? I like plans and knowing what’s coming next. I don’t like surprises. The thought of spontaneous road trips makes me uncomfortable. I’d rather know where we’re headed and what to expect. Should I be more adventurous? Everyone says it’s good to step out of your comfort zone but my comfort zone is so cozy. And the little things get me too. Like the way someone responds to me in a group chat. Did I say something wrong? I can reread those messages a hundred times, overanalyzing every word. I think I should just say what’s on my mind sometimes, but instead, I worry it will come out wrong. I don’t want to stir the pot. I just want peaceful interactions. I feel like I’m always watching, always observing, always aware of the dynamics around me. Maybe I should write it all down instead of bottling it up. Journaling could be a good outlet—but then I’d worry someone would read it. Okay, but seriously, I need to make that email happen. I should do it before I forget again.

Okay so, my phone’s buzzing again, probably another group chat notification. I really should mute that but I can’t help but want to stay connected. It’s like this constant stream of energy flowing through my day—it’s exhilarating! But then sometimes I feel like it’s too much. Maybe I shouldn’t have agreed to go out every weekend but what if I miss something? I don’t want to be left out, and social events are where I really thrive, you know? Everybody’s laughing and connecting, and I just can’t get enough of it. Lately, I’ve noticed I’m more eager than usual to chat with everyone, even strangers at the bus stop or the library. Random compliments pop out of nowhere; I told a guy in class that his backpack had cool patches and he lit up like I gave him a gift. I mean, it feels great to share that positivity but I sometimes worry if I’m coming off too strong. Speaking of class, oh my god, don’t even get me started on group projects! I love collaborating, the energy of brainstorming ideas is just unmatched. But then one member doesn’t pull their weight, and it drives me nuts! I can’t stand slackers, and it gets my nerves all worked up. I overthink and worry that it’ll affect my grade, which, okay, might sound a bit neurotic, but I can’t help it! I’ve worked so hard to maintain a solid GPA. It’s this constant balancing act—wanting to be friendly but also wanting everyone to contribute. Sometimes I end up taking control to make sure we stay on track, but it’s a delicate dance between leading and letting others do their part. I guess I do love a good challenge though, especially with my type-A personality pushing me to make everything perfect. I just hope they still like me afterward! People’s opinions matter a lot to me—I know they shouldn’t weigh so heavily, but I’m always second-guessing how I come off in conversations. Then there’s my family. My mom keeps calling, checking up on me, and honestly, I love it. She worries about my late nights studying or going out, but I promise her I’m doing okay. It’s so reassuring to feel that connection, but I do feel a tinge of guilt for making her worry. They’ve sacrificed so much for me to be here, a part of me just wants to make them proud. My little sister is going through high school and I’m always sending her advice, like how to fit in and be yourself at the same time. She’s got a great personality; I just hope she doesn’t doubt herself like I sometimes do. I wish I had her fearless spirit when I was that age. And then I remember how it felt to be in those chaotic lunchroom scenes, trying to find a place to sit. Okay, but really, friendships are everything to me. I have this small but close-knit circle, and our group chats are always buzzing. We share our ups and downs, and I love hearing about everyone’s lives. I can’t help but dive deep into their feelings, making sure they know I’m there for them. Every time someone opens up about their struggles, I feel the weight of their emotions and want to wrap them in a warm hug. I guess my agreeableness means I can’t stand seeing anyone sad, so I’m always pushing for positive vibes and solutions. I’m like a cheerleader, sometimes I need to chill but I can’t help it! But then there are moments of overwhelming anxiety. My mind races about deadlines and what ifs? What if I don’t finish my paper on time? Or worse, what if it’s not good enough? I scroll through study tips and end up reading so many articles, I get lost in a sea of opinions. It makes my head spin sometimes, like stop, there’s too much information! But I can’t resist learning, I’m constantly curious about everything—new places to visit, cultures to explore, ideas to discuss. I love museums and galleries, and I’m always trying to convince my friends to go with me. I thrive on those experiences! Just thinking about trying new food or going out to an art event makes my heart race with excitement. I want to experience everything and everyone, share stories, learn, grow—I’m just so ready to embrace all this beautiful chaos! And it’s all so fast-paced, this whirlwind of friendships and worries and dinners and tests. I mean, life is happening all around me, and I want to soak it in but oh my god, sometimes I feel like I’m running a marathon, never catching my breath. But then I see a smile or hear a joke and I remember why I love it so much. There’s a beautiful messiness to everything; it’s like a wild adventure and I want to be in every moment. So, what’s next? New friendships, new experiences, and let’s figure out this whole life thing together.

People are so fascinating. I mean, everyone has their own story, right? Like I met this guy at a party last week. He had the wildest hair, like a tornado of curls, and he was telling me all about his bizarre travel experiences. Oh, how I love hearing about the weird places people go and the strange things they encounter. Forget the usual touristy stuff—give me the crazy stories! He said he once got chased by a wild boar in Italy. Can you imagine that? A wild boar! I can’t even keep a cactus alive, and he’s out there fending off animals. So, anyway, I started thinking about all the places I want to go. Bali is definitely on my list, but I wonder if it’s really as great as everyone says. I saw some pictures, and it looks heavenly, but do pictures ever really capture the vibe? I want to feel it, the energy, the sweat, the vibrant cultures, the hustle and bustle—it all sounds perfect! Then again, what if it’s overrated? I mean, they have those Instagram spots, and then I wonder if everyone is just there for the ‘Gram. I can't stand when people are just doing something for social media. Speaking of which, I need to check my followers. Wait, no, I shouldn’t get sucked into that right now. I find myself scrolling for ages. I hate it when I get caught in the loop. But then, if I don’t check, what if I miss out on something important? Ugh, the pressure! Do you think anyone ever feels the same kind of anxiety? I mean, everyone has their demons, right? Like I get all jittery sometimes, the most random things make my heart race. My phone dings, and I go into panic mode like it’s the end of the world. Did I miss something? Or did someone post something that would ruin my day? I have to remind myself to breathe. Sometimes I feel like a total mess. But isn’t it weird that feeling anxious somehow makes me want to reach out more? I’ll send texts to friends even when I’m feeling on edge, like I need to talk about the little things that fluster me. Oh, this one friend of mine always says she loves my spur-of-the-moment texts. I’m like, “Girl, I can’t help it!” I just need to share my thoughts, even if they’re a jumbled mess. And she’s always so understanding. I value that so much. Connecting with people helps, right? I love hearing their perspectives. It’s like a little comfort blanket against the chaos of life. But sometimes, when I’m overwhelmed, I overthink everything. It’s like my mind goes into overdrive, racing through every little possibility. What if someone doesn’t like me? What if I said the wrong thing at that last party? What if I make a fool of myself in class tomorrow? I wish I could just relax. To be honest, I envy people who are all chill and collected. How do they do that? Do they have some secret elixir to keep the worries away? I want a sip of that! Maybe it’s in a fancy bottle I haven’t found yet. Then I remember the study I read about extroverts and how they thrive in social situations. I mean, that’s me! I can chat up anyone, anywhere. But those moments of self-doubt? Ugh. That’s the tricky part. I want to be the life of the party but with zero insecurities. I’m great at rallying people together, but then I’m always second-guessing myself. It makes me laugh, though. There’s something endearing about the messiness of it all. Maybe I should just embrace it, like, “Hey everyone, I’m a bundle of nerves wrapped in enthusiasm!” And what’s with the sudden rain? It’s like the universe knows I need a distraction. I could dive into Netflix, but that whole “one more episode” thing gets dangerous. I start with one episode of a new show and suddenly it’s 2 AM, and I have regrets. Last Friday, I watched that new series about the guy who keeps reliving the same day. So relatable! I feel like I relive my anxious moments on repeat - overthinking, stressing, then reaching out. It loops and starts again! On the bright side, when I’m not racing thoughts, I’m full of ideas! Inspiration strikes at the weirdest places. Like when I’m brushing my teeth or waiting for my microwave burrito to finish. Can you believe that? I’ll suddenly think of a million things I want to do, places I want to visit, people I want to talk to. Who knew a burrito could spark such creativity? I guess it’s all part of the whirlpool that is my brain. Constantly spinning, bubbling with energy and thoughts that sometimes collide dramatically. It’s a wild ride, but I wouldn’t trade it for anything.

Okay, so I was just thinking about how everyone always seems to be so social and extroverted, like they thrive in groups and parties and I’m just here like can we not? I mean sometimes the noise is just too much. I wish I could just be in my own head in my cozy little bubble but then the world pulls me out of it and expects me to interact. I can’t do small talk. What even is small talk? I’d rather discuss existential dread or why people think pineapple belongs on pizza. Also, the whole vibe of college is so overwhelming, like the constant pressure to socialize, to fit in. I don’t know how people do it without losing their minds. I feel like I’m constantly on high alert, the anxiety just bubbling underneath the surface waiting to pop. And then I get these intrusive thoughts, like am I even supposed to be here, is this really what I want? I could be home reading or researching something obscure on the internet. Why do we have to conform? I feel like I see every flaw in myself, and I can’t help but overthink every interaction. Did I say the right thing? Did I laugh too loud? Did I offend someone? Probably, I’m probably just a walking mistake. I should be working on that essay due next week, but I’ve got this feeling like I just can’t focus. I want everything to be perfect but then I end up frozen because what if it’s not perfect? And then time flies by and nothing gets done. I keep a planner, I write everything down, color code my tasks. It helps, but it also feels like a crutch sometimes. Like I’m so diligent and I crave order but it’s never enough. The pressure to succeed looms large over me, and if I don’t meet my own expectations, it’s catastrophic. I know that’s a lot of drama, but you know what? That’s where my mind goes. I have this intense curiosity about everything, though, that’s the paradox. Like I’ll dive deep into some random topic, spend hours reading and absorbing facts, but then it’s like why can’t I channel that same focus into something productive that actually matters like studying for finals or cleaning my room? My room is a mess right now, so cluttered. I can’t work in chaos, but then I get too overwhelmed to clean, it’s like this vicious cycle. I think about the future a lot too, where will I end up? Can I really make it in my field? The thought makes my stomach twist. I want to explore, I want to travel, and discover. But every time I think about actually doing it, there’s this little voice that whispers “You’re not brave enough for this.” It’s such a conflicting space in my head, you know? Open to new experiences but so afraid of taking the plunge. I see my friends all going out, doing things, and I’m just sitting back in the corner, like why can’t I just join them? I feel so out of sync sometimes, like I’m watching a movie where everyone else is starring and I’m just this background character that doesn’t really matter. I’ve started to enjoy writing though, which is a good outlet. There’s something cathartic about putting my thoughts down, unfiltered. I just wish I could get these thoughts out quicker without getting stuck in my head. But then I wonder, who really cares what I have to say, anyway? It’s hard to reconcile the urge for connection with the fear of judgment. I often feel like I can’t trust people either. That low agreeableness, I guess? I have this weird sense that people have ulterior motives. It makes me hesitant to open up, and then I end up feeling so isolated. It’s a downward spiral of wanting to reach out but pulling back because of past experiences. I have built these walls, and sometimes I just don’t know how to knock them down. Maybe it’ll get easier. Maybe if I keep pushing myself, I’ll find that balance between being cautious and being open. Every interaction feels like a battle, and I’m just trying to survive. But I also think that’s part of being human, right? This constant struggle to connect and comprehend. I keep coming back to the things I love—books, art, knowledge. They feel safer. And I wonder if they’ll always be there for me, in the moments when the world feels too chaotic. Like a constant, reliable friend. It's comforting in a way. Just the thought of endless possibilities fuels me, even when the anxiety threatens to overtake it. It's strange how that works. It's like I can be both anxious and curious at the same time, both wanting to retreat and wanting to explore.

Okay so I've been thinking about how exhausting it is to even go to class lately. Just sit there and listen to everyone chatter like it's the most natural thing in the world. My mind wanders. What are they even talking about? Probably something mundane. Their outfits, the new café that opened. I can never seem to join in. I keep my thoughts inside. It’s easier that way. I mean, I guess I could ask someone about their weekend, but why? I'm not really interested. I don't want to feign interest, that seems so fake. They might be nice, but I don’t need their validation. I wish I could just be like, "Hey, your shoes are cool" and leave it at that, but my brain says "no, what if they think you’re weird?" Anxiety. It's a constant buzz in the background like white noise. Sometimes I feel like I’m in a bubble and they’re all living outside of it. Like I’m watching a movie, and I’m the only one who knows the plot twists. Why do people want to connect so much? It’s exhausting. I’d rather explore ideas than talk about what we did last weekend. The more I think of it, the more I realize I hate small talk. It feels pointless. You just go in circles asking questions that no one cares about. “How’s your day?” “Good, and you?” It’s all scripted. I want to shout sometimes, “Can we just talk about the universe or why we exist?” But that would scare them away. So I just nod and smile, while my brain goes on tangents about life and the cosmos and how everything is so interconnected in this chaotic way. I think about the theories of philosophers and what they thought about emotion and existence. Can you just imagine sitting with Socrates, drinking coffee and having an intellectual debate while everyone else is outside arguing about sports or reality TV? I’d feel so much more at home there. But would I still be anxious? Probably. My mind is always racing, thinking about one million things at once, and then there’s that ping of self-doubt. Am I smart enough? Do I even know what I’m talking about? Does it matter? Maybe it doesn't. It’s just thoughts, but they chase each other like a bunch of kids in a playground, all screaming. It's a loud playground in my head, and I can’t get off the merry-go-round. I can’t help but notice how everyone else seems to have it together. They talk, they laugh, they interact like they’re all on some secret script that I wasn’t given. I mean, how do they choose where to go? How do they pick their friends? I had a friend once and it was great for a while, but then it got complicated. I overthought things. Did I say something wrong? Did I take up too much space in conversation? Did they even like me? I overanalyze to the point where I can’t even remember why we haven’t talked in months. And then I’m left with this empty feeling, like I’m drifting, and I think to myself that maybe I just prefer being alone. Reading, thinking, absorbing everything quietly. But who am I kidding? Sometimes the loneliness creeps in. Nights alone with just my thoughts and sometimes they feel like too much. I put on music to drown it out but the lyrics resonate too much. Like, “Hello darkness, my old friend.” Yeah, that’s where I go sometimes. I should probably get outside more. I read about how nature can help with anxiety but the thought of being around people outside is just as daunting. What if someone sits too close to me? What if I can’t find a spot to immerse myself in a book? What if I stand there and freeze up? This is real life now, and the world doesn't make sense. Sometimes I think I need a companion, someone who gets it—but how do I find that person? It feels like trying to find a needle in a haystack. There’s probably a million different people thinking about their own existential crisis. I get it. And what if we met? Would we even talk about it, or would we just stare at the ground hoping someone else would break the silence? I want to travel. See new places. Experience cultures, but then I think, "What if they don’t like me?" or “What if I can’t communicate?” I can think about all the art I want to see, the landscapes that take my breath away, but I get sucked back into my own head. It’s like a constant tug-of-war with my own desires. Why can’t I just let go? Why do I have to analyze every thought until it’s a tangled mess? Oh, the thoughts are racing now. Maybe I should just breathe. Sometimes I forget that I can just breathe. In and out. But then a new thought pops up, and the cycle continues.

Okay so I just thought about how I really like my planner. I think it’s weird but satisfying to put everything in there. Like, when I fill in the days with tasks, it gives me this weird sense of control. I write down everything, from classes to grocery shopping to “don’t forget to call mom.” It’s nice. I like knowing exactly what I need to do. I’m not one for surprises—surprises stress me out. I guess that’s why I stick to my routine. It’s comfortable. I like the familiar. Routines are like little anchors. Speaking of routines, I always have a hard time deciding what to have for breakfast. I go back and forth between oatmeal and toast. Oatmeal sounds healthy and wholesome, but toast is just so much easier most morning. I like to keep it simple. And peanut butter. I love peanut butter. It’s probably my favorite spread. I can eat it with just about anything. I’m thinking about making a peanut butter banana toast soon. I think I have bananas that are just ripe enough. Then I think about how bananas are like perfect little packages of energy. I should probably eat more fruit. It’s just that fruit is such a hassle sometimes—getting it, washing it, cutting it up. I always think “I’ll do it later,” but later never comes, does it? That's okay though. I kind of like the predictability of my meals. I can't imagine eating something out of the blue. That’s why I never try new places to eat. I just stick to my favorites, and honestly, I like it that way. Familiar faces at the same old diner, where everyone knows your order. And while I’m on the topic of food, I just remembered how my roommate keeps bringing home those weird snacks. You know the ones that are just odd combinations—like pickles wrapped in something or spicy chips? I appreciate her adventurous spirit, I really do. I just can't indulge in those kinds of things. I feel anxious even thinking about it. Comfort food is my jam. Like mac and cheese—what’s more comforting than that? I can whip up a box of it without even thinking. Everything’s so easy—it gives me peace of mind. Okay but social stuff…I was at this group study session last week. I mean, I sort of dread those things, honestly. It’s not that I don’t like people. I do. Really. I just prefer one-on-one. In groups, there’s always the chatter, and it’s hard for me to focus. I end up just nodding along while trying to stay engaged, but my mind drifts. I think about what I need to get done, how long until I can go back to my cozy corner and just read or plan. I always feel like I’m the glue holding the group together, trying to keep conversation going when there’s a lull. It’s oddly burdensome. Yet, I always end up helping everyone else, and that’s okay. I mean I like being agreeable and helping out, it’s just that I wish everyone would stop looking at me to lead things. I’m not a leader; I’m just organized and want things to go smoothly. Coordinating the study group was fine, but I just want to blend in next time, not be pinned under the spotlight. I wish people understood that I’m not a loud person. I don’t thrive on attention. I fear it sometimes. It’s hard because it seems like everyone around me is always so excited and energized, and I’m more like “let’s just do our work and get out of here.” Then there’s the issue of friendships. I like my friends. I do. They’re reliable and sweet, and they include me in things, but I sometimes feel like I’m just along for the ride. I’m always agreeing to things because I don’t want to disappoint them, but deep down, I’m just hoping we can play it low-key. I like going to the same coffee shop, sitting in the same corner, having the same discussions about the same topics. I’m predictable like that. I find comfort in the small things, in the good old routines. My parents used to say that I was a creature of habit, and I guess they were right. But it’s not that I’m boring; I just appreciate stability more than novelty. I wonder if that limits my experiences, but it’s just who I am. I can’t be bothered by all the waves of unpredictability. Life feels overwhelming as it is. At least I know my morning coffee will always be a calm, warm embrace.

So, I was thinking about the time I met that one girl at the party. I barely said two words to her, just listened while she went on about her travels. She had been to like six different countries in four months. How do people do that, just pick up and go? It sounds amazing, but I feel this mix of anxiety and excitement when I think about it, and I worry about all the little things, like tickets and where to stay and what if I get lost or what if I don’t have the right clothes or get sick or— ugh, it’s exhausting just thinking about it. I wish I could just be more carefree but then I get really stuck in my head like a hamster on a wheel, you know? And then my mind wanders to social situations and sometimes I'm fine with sitting quietly but other times I feel this pressure to blend in and be interesting like I’m not enough just being me. Little things like my favorite mug - it’s chipped and has a weird design but it feels comfy in my hand like an old friend. I should probably get rid of it but that’s hard to do, right? It’s just a stupid mug but it brings a sort of comfort, the kind that reminds me of the quiet mornings where I’d drink tea and read while everyone else seemed to have their lives together. I think about how everyone wants to be liked and I want that too but also I’m terrified of disappointing people, like what if they don’t like me? Or worse, what if they find me boring? I hate small talk, like what's the point. Why can’t we just jump right into deep conversations and existential stuff? I could do that all day. I’d rather share my thoughts on the meaning of life or what dreams mean even if I don’t have answers but nobody asks. Sometimes I feel like I'm just floating through my days, not really grounded anywhere, like I could just drift off. Classes feel strange, and sitting there trying to focus when my mind darts around every few seconds, flitting from one thought to another like a butterfly. Right now, I’m obsessing over the fact that I haven’t written enough on my paper and I keep telling myself it’s okay, but then I think about my professor and her eyes narrowing in disappointment if I don’t do well. It fills me with dread. I wish I could just let it go but I can't, the anxiety just bubbles and boils. And speaking of papers, how cool would it be to write about random things, like how we have all these social norms that dictate how we interact? There’s a part of me that wants to upend those norms, break free and say something ridiculous, but then I worry everyone would just stare or laugh awkwardly and I’d feel like I’d messed it all up. I don’t even want to be the center of attention, just existing in a space feels heavy enough. It doesn’t help that I’m such a people pleaser. I want everyone to be okay, I want to help but I struggle with my own issues. I remember this time I was volunteering and tried to uplift a friend who was down but I felt so inadequate. Like, what could I possibly say that would help? I just wanted to fix everything and make it all better for them. Then I got home and couldn’t shake the weight of it, so I wrote a poem about feeling overwhelmed with caring for others when I can barely take care of myself. And my mind is racing again. I have this idea that maybe I should draw more or paint. I used to enjoy those things. What happened? I can’t even remember the last time I picked up a brush or pencil, maybe there’s something in that, like maybe art is the outlet I need to express all this bottled chaos. Can I even call it chaos if it’s just thoughts? Or is that being too dramatic? I think about how I want to be open to experiences and I guess that’s a positive, right? Opening up is scary but also necessary. Oh! I just remembered that movie I watched last week about time travel and how the character wrestled with making choices and their ripple effects. I think I could talk about that forever, how choices shape us, how we can learn from mistakes but sometimes my choices make me second-guess everything. If I just had answers or a little more courage. There’s so much beauty in uncertainty but also so much discomfort. I wonder if we ever really find certainty, or if life is just one big question mark, like those endless math problems nobody wants to solve. I really need to get my life together, figure out how to balance my fears and my openness to new experiences without getting overwhelmed. I feel like I’m on the brink of something, like a transformation or something just beneath the surface. But there’s that voice reminding me, “What if you fail?” And it’s hard to push it away.

so I was thinking about how people always talk about being spontaneous like it’s this great thing but honestly I don’t see the appeal if I know I can just plan everything out it makes life so much easier and predictable like why would I want to just jump into a car and drive somewhere without knowing where I’m going or what I’ll do when I get there I don’t even like surprises I like knowing exactly what to expect it’s less stressful that way people say that surprises are fun like for birthdays or whatever but if I’m not the one planning it then what's the point it might not be fun at all and I’ve seen some pretty cringey surprise parties in movies or just in life where it goes all wrong someone gets upset or embarrassed and then what’s the point of that plus I hate parties I’d much rather sit alone in my room reading or watching something familiar that I know I like why waste time on small talk with strangers or acquaintances I mean who even cares about their weekend or their job or whatever just feels pointless why can’t we all just agree to not talk unless it’s necessary just get straight to the point efficiency that’s how I see it. Also, people can be really dumb sometimes like during group projects in classes why do they want to make it so complicated instead of just dividing the work logically and getting it done efficiently they have to throw in all these ideas like it’s some sort of brainstorming session and I’m just there like can we just stick to the plan and get this over with but no they want to be all touchy-feely about teamwork and collaboration it’s frustrating because I don’t care about everyone’s feelings just do the work and let’s move on I can’t stand when someone tries to share their feelings about the project or something that doesn’t even matter just focus on the end goal it’s like they’re trying to connect but I don’t need that connection stuff makes me feel awkward like what do you even say back to that I mean I can manage a polite nod or whatever but deep down all I’m thinking is just let’s finish this and get out of here. Sometimes I think about how people seem to thrive on social interactions like it gives them energy but I don’t get it I feel drained after even short interactions especially with people I don’t know I can’t even focus afterward I need time to recharge and it’s always the same like going to class and people start conversations like why don’t they just focus on the lecture instead of chatting like it’s their last chance to make friends or something I’m thinking about how I can just listen to the professor and take notes then go home and do my own thing why does everyone have to be so chatty it’s exhausting it’s okay for them but for me it’s like running a marathon just listening to them talk about their lives when I don’t care like I have my own life I don’t need details about someone else’s breakfast or whatever it’s just not interesting I’m not judging them but also I don’t get how people can act like that. Anyway speaking of classes I really hate when they have group discussions like what’s the point we all read the same thing why can’t we just write down our thoughts and hand them in instead of sitting around talking about it like I can’t stand listening to someone who just repeats the same thing everyone else has said it’s just a waste of my time and I could be doing something more productive like scrolling through my phone or even napping which would be more enjoyable than listening to someone who thinks they have a unique perspective but it’s all pretty much the same as everyone else it’s boring and I just don’t get why teachers think this is a good idea or why students go along with it like sheep following a shepherd. And then there’s the whole thing about hobbies and interests like when people talk about their passions but do I seem passionate about anything that requires like time and effort and money to pursue I mean I get that people have their interests but when they talk about them it’s like they expect everyone to share their enthusiasm and I’m not interested in joining clubs or whatever I don’t want to spend my free time doing stuff with other people I’d rather be on my own doing something that I know I can enjoy without anyone hovering around me asking me questions I don’t care what other people think about what I like I just want to do my own thing and it seems like such a chore to have to explain myself to others. I just wish there was a way to function in a society without all the expectations of being social or agreeable I mean can’t we just acknowledge that some people prefer solitude without making it a big deal or assuming I need to work on being more outgoing or that I’m missing out on something big I get that for them but I’m fine with my routine it’s comfortable and predictable and the thought of shaking things up even a little bit is stressful I can’t deal with the chaos I’m much more inclined to stick to what I know and that’s okay I guess.

It's 2:37 PM and I’m just thinking about how every time I try to make plans, my mind goes straight to the what-ifs. What if it rains? What if nobody shows up? What if I annoy them? I mean, it’s even hard to decide on a place to eat with friends like, there’s just so many options and what if I pick the wrong one? They’ll think I’m weird for liking that place or something. All those quirky little spots like the ramen shop, which I love but only if the broth is right, what if it’s too salty today? I remember last week when I went to the bookstore. I always feel so overwhelmed by the selection. I love books though, stories, non-fiction, poetry, everything really. I get this rush when I find a section I love but then I’m just standing there, squeezing the spine of the book like if I hold it tight enough it’ll give me courage. Why is it so hard to just browse? There’s so much noise in my head, like, “You’ll never read all these, why are you even here?” Sometimes I feel like I’m more comfortable in the pages of a book than talking to actual people. Books don't judge me. And then I think of my friends. They’re great but sometimes I feel so out of place. They’re all laughing and joking and I’m just there trying to keep up, nodding and hoping they don’t notice I’m not adding anything meaningful to the conversation. I want to be funny too, witty and charming, but my thoughts race ahead and I just freeze. Like this one time we were joking about that ridiculous movie we watched. I wanted to say something clever, but all that came out was me mumbling about the plot holes, which nobody cared about, of course. I felt stupid. I’m always trying to be agreeable though, maybe excessively so, always saying yes to things I don’t even want to do. Like that last group project. I ended up taking charge which is fine, I guess. I like organizing things, making lists, and ensuring everything runs smoothly. It felt good to cross things off a to-do list, but I ended up doing all the work and it wasn’t even appreciated. Why do I try so hard? I get anxious just thinking about it. It’s like I don’t want to let anyone down. I hate conflict. I’d rather sit through a boring lecture than deal with a disagreement. I remember a disagreement with my roommate over the volume of the music. She thought I should just deal with it because it was her time to unwind. I just sat there, feeling my heart race as I tried to figure out how to express my feelings without making it awkward, like why can’t I just say “Hey can you turn it down a bit?” Why did I have to overthink it? Eventually, I just put my headphones on and pretended to be immersed in my own world. The world outside feels chaotic, everyone rushing, connecting, and talking. I can’t keep up. It’s like being at a party and you stand there awkwardly holding a drink, watching everyone have fun. I’ve seen videos of parties that look so lively but in reality, I just want to curl up at home with my sketchbook. I love to draw, and I can spend hours just doodling whatever comes to mind, some abstract shapes, or trying to replicate a painting I saw once. It’s comforting and it drowns out the noise. I wonder if that means I’m lonely. Am I really? I don’t mind the solitude, not always, but sometimes I feel this pang of wanting to connect, like why can’t I just take that leap? I think about the people I genuinely connect with, the deep conversations we have about life or art and it feels so fulfilling, but those moments are rare. I guess I need to be more open, more willing to step outside of my head. But then again, maybe I’m too open sometimes. I let every little thing affect me. I find myself still ruminating over a comment someone made weeks ago, dissecting it like an old school essay. It just plays in my mind like a broken record and I get frustrated with myself. How do I just let go? I know I need a balance, but how? I feel like my thoughts are all over the place, just stringing together random ideas that don’t make sense. I wonder if anyone ever feels this way. Just swirling thoughts and emotions, trying to make sense of it all. It’s exhausting yet I can’t help but reflect and analyze every moment. Maybe I need to just breathe. Take deep breaths. Stop caring so much and just live a little. Okay, it’s 2:56 PM now. Almost time’s up. I hope this makes some sense. I guess writing it all down helps clear the fog in my head a bit. I’ll try to be more spontaneous next time.

Okay so I just remembered that one time I went to that concert last summer. It was like a whirlwind of energy, everyone crammed in, the music thumping and the lights flashing. There was this girl next to me, and I swear we became best friends in that one night. I don’t even know her name but we were singing along to every song and dancing like we were the only two people in the world. The vibe was just pure happiness, you know? Everyone was so open and free-spirited. I love how people can come together like that, strangers who just vibe off each other even if it’s for a couple of hours. Speaking of concerts, there’s another one coming up next month, I should definitely go, maybe text a few people. I wonder if anyone's still around from last year’s crew? I remember we all went out for tacos after that show, and we talked about everything. I was so full of beans that night, I was convinced I could do anything, like travel the world on a whim or take up a new hobby or maybe just go skydiving next week. I don’t get why people are so scared of trying new things. Life’s too short! Like why not just jump? It’s so freeing—oh man I miss those random road trips too. Spontaneous drives with no destination, just the open road and the music blasting, singing along to our favorite tracks until we lost our voices. I should really plan something like that again. I wonder if my friends would be down for that. Maybe we should just pack some snacks, grab a cooler, and head down to the beach. I could totally spend an entire day just lounging in the sun, having beach games, and making sandcastles. Or wait, maybe not sandcastles, we could do something more ridiculous like a competition for the tallest sand structure or something. The crazier the better! I love how silly we can get when we just let loose and not think about all the responsibilities hanging over our heads. What even are those things like ‘adulting’? There’s always too much on the to-do list, but whatever, let’s just kick back and enjoy the moment. Back to that girl at the concert... She had this amazing energy, and it was contagious. I truly believe that some people just radiate good vibes and it’s wonderful to be around them. I often find myself gravitating toward those types—the ones who see the glass as half full, who can make the best of any situation. I think it’s interesting how personality types interact. You know, those little dynamics we have with everyone. Like, I can’t stand when someone brings a negative vibe into the group. Like, come on, lighten up! It’s all about having a good time and making memories. I just think the world has enough seriousness without us adding to it. I wonder if I could make a career out of connecting with people like that, the ones who inspire, uplift, and encourage others. I don’t know what I’d do exactly, maybe something in event planning or community outreach? I could totally see myself bringing people together to create experiences that matter. I’d love to organize festivals or local gatherings that could foster relationships. It’s all about connection, right? It’s funny how sometimes strangers can feel closer than family, especially if you find common ground. Oh, and that brings me to food. I just had the most amazing burrito yesterday from that taco truck nearby. There's something about street food that's just so good, like how can they pack so much flavor into a tiny tortilla? I always tell myself I’ll try new things but I end up getting the same burrito again and again. But then again, if it ain’t broke don’t fix it. I mean, I could be adventurous and get different stuff but there’s comfort in the familiar. Maybe I’ll finally branch out and try that vegan option next time. And oh! I need to remember to sign up for that art class I’ve been eyeing. It just sounds like a great chance to meet new people and create something. I love the idea of being messy with paint and having no real expectations—just pure experimentation. That’s all I really want, to enjoy the process rather than focus on the outcome. Really can’t wait for all this fun to happen—everything just feels right when I’m outside exploring new things or meeting different people. I should text some friends now, maybe even plan a little gathering, bring everyone together to share all the fun and ideas flying around. Who knows what adventures await? Life is too short for boredom, so let’s make some memories!

The clock says 3:14 PM. I wonder why it feels like time is stretching today, like I'm in a slow-motion scene but there's no actual event happening. I can hear my roommate in the other room, laughing at something on YouTube. I find joy in that, the echo of happiness, even just from a distance. It’s nice to be around someone who radiates positivity without requiring me to interact too much. I guess that’s just how I am. Let’s see, I’ve been thinking a lot about how people connect. Like, some people thrive off being around others; I mean, I can enjoy it sometimes but too much socializing drains me. I often prefer one-on-one conversations where I can really dig deep into thoughts, ideas, anything really. I often ponder about the whole concept of “networking” in college. It’s so… transactional for many, like “I’ll scratch your back if you scratch mine.” That smells nice. My roommate is cooking. It reminds me of home-cooked meals. My mom always put her heart into cooking, like she wanted to nourish us not just physically but emotionally too. There’s something comforting about the smell of garlic and onions sautéing. Food is such an experience; it brings back memories of family dinners, laughter cutting through the clattering of dishes, the warmth of being together. Then I think about how food can be such a bridge between people, especially in a diverse college environment. Everyone has different traditions and flavors they bring to the table, literally and figuratively. I wonder how people find their place in the grand scheme of things. My major is psychology; I’m fascinated by all the layers of human behavior. A friend suggested I try meditation to keep my focus sharp, relieve that occasional racing mind of mine. It sounds appealing, but I worry I’d just end up thinking about too much stuff, like the laundry I need to do or that book I haven’t finished reading. The one about mindfulness—what a concept, right? Being present sounds so intriguing, but I guess I’ve always been a future-oriented person, always planning. I like my checklists; even just writing one feels satisfying. Sometimes I feel like everything has to be perfect but then I also embrace spontaneity. It’s weird. I want to know how the universe works but I also want to see what happens if I take a different route home. Like, walking through the park instead of rushing back to the dorm. I saw a family of ducks last week, just waddling along the path without a care in the world. They seemed so content, just existing. What about the people who ignore the beauty around them? It’s sad in a way. I want to understand their perspective. Do they see it but don’t process it? Or do they just not care? It’s fascinating to think about the layers of experience we all have. And yet, we so often settle into patterns where we forget to stop and appreciate things, the little details. Speaking of little details, I need to start concentrating more on my assignments. I put so much effort into them; I want them to be good. I put my heart and soul into my psychology projects, analyzing theories, applying them to real-life situations. I love the way psychology can explain almost everything. The mind is a labyrinth, and I’m determined to explore all its corners, no matter how dark or tangled they may be. And then there’s that nagging voice in my head about career paths. What if I want to go into research instead of clinical work? Would that be disappointing for my family? They’ve always supported my choices but it’s hard to imagine breaking the mold when I’ve been so focused on helping others directly. Ugh, decisions! I think I’d like to travel someday. Explore different cultures, taste various foods, meet people with completely different worldviews. There’s so much out there beyond my little bubble! I read this one article about a psychologist who traveled to different countries, studying their mental health practices and how community impacts wellbeing. That makes so much sense to me. It’s all interconnected, isn’t it? The way we treat each other, how we support one another. I feel like I naturally want to help; it’s just part of who I am. Maybe that’s why I like volunteering at the campus food bank. Even for a few hours each week, it feels like I’m making a small difference, connecting with people, sharing smiles even if they’re just brief interactions. I wonder how these connections shape me. I mean, I learn so much from everyone I meet, their stories, their struggles. It’s like every interaction is a little lesson, a piece of a huge puzzle that I’m trying to put together. My life is this ongoing exploration, and I guess that’s what keeps it exciting.

That coffee cup in front of me is really something, I mean the way it sits there like a loyal dog waiting for its owner to fill it up again, just one more time. Sometimes I feel like I can drink coffee all day, and there’s nothing better than that first sip in the morning. It’s like, yes, I’m alive, ready to tackle the day. I can’t help but think about how I like to plan everything out. My friends tease me about it, but I think it keeps me organized, like my planner is my best friend or something. I like color-coding stuff; it gives me a sense of control, you know? The way a day should flow – breakfast, class, studying, maybe a workout, and definitely hanging out with friends later. I can’t do spontaneous; it just gives me anxiety. What if something goes wrong? Okay, so last week, we had a group project, and I went in thinking we could knock it out of the park if I organized it right. Everyone else was like, “Let’s just wing it!” and I’m like, “No! We have to write this out and assign roles.” It felt like herding cats, trying to get people to focus, but it worked! At the end of the day, all of us got an A! Totally worth the stress of dealing with distractions. We celebrated at a local place with burgers and fries. I talked a lot that night, mostly about how proud I was of everyone. I love those moments when things come together. It makes the effort worth it. Then there’s my roommate, Lisa, who’s super chill, a free spirit. She’s always saying, “Let’s go on an adventure!” and I’m like, “To where?” Spontaneity is her middle name, and while I admire that – seriously, I do! – it’s exhausting. Like, I’m down for adventures if they’re planned out. I guess I enjoy knowing what’s on the horizon, it gives me that little rush of anticipation. The thrill when you know what’s coming next. You can’t just throw me in a car and expect me to be okay. Can you imagine? “Hey, we’re driving to the beach!” and I’m sitting there panicking because I forgot my sunscreen, or I didn’t bring a towel! It’s funny how my circle is always changing, but the core people? They’re consistent. I think I naturally gravitate towards other people who like how I organize stuff. My friends call me ‘the planner,’ and that makes me happy! I like being the one to initiate plans. Movie night? I’ll pick a theme! Potluck? I have recipes in mind. It’s just a part of me, I guess, to create these moments. But I also love hearing what others think. I mean, we can turn a simple gathering into a big deal with a few decorations and good food. I’m all about the teamwork. Nobody likes a party pooper! Speaking of parties, I attended this one last month. Man, the energy was wild. I was in my element. I knew everyone there, which made it super easy to jump from group to group. I love those nights when you can just connect and chat with so many different people. I think being friendly just opens up doors; it’s like a skill everyone should have. You go in and smile, and suddenly, you’re talking about your favorite ice cream flavor with a stranger! How cool is that? But it’s weird, too, how sometimes I just don't understand people. Like, when they complain about absolutely everything or just show too much negativity? It baffles me! I mean, life can be tough, but there’s so much good out there. I’m the type of person who will always look on the bright side, even if it seems bleak. Let’s say you bomb a test. Yeah, that sucks, but hey, there’s always a retake! And I’ll be there to help you study. I love being supportive, just lifting others up when they need it. Now, what’s the deal with all those TikTok trends? I get there’s creativity involved, but are people really enjoying themselves or just trying to keep up with something? If that’s what fun has become based on likes and shares, wow. I’d rather have a board game night where we can actually talk and laugh rather than scroll endlessly. It’s like, let’s cherish those moments instead, right? Deep conversations over a game of Scrabble beat a thousand likes any day! I think about my future a lot. I want to make an impact, maybe in social work or something that helps others. My parents always instilled that in me, the value of helping. It’s rewarding, and honestly? I want to enjoy what I do. I think that’s what keeps me motivated. I hope I can inspire people, just like how planning inspires me to make the most of every moment. But who knows? It’s an adventure in itself, right?

What’s that smell? Oh, right, that leftover pizza in the fridge. I should probably throw that out but then again maybe it’s still good I mean there’s a chance I’d eat it. Probably not though, the thought of mold is just gross. Sometimes I wonder if I should care more about food safety or just live dangerously. Maybe I should have been more adventurous, whatever that even means, I don’t know. People talk about stepping out of your comfort zone but what if my comfort zone is really where it’s at for me? Like when I’m there scrolling through Reddit until I lose track of time and then I realize I should really be studying for that one class I’m failing. Ugh, statistics, I hate how the professor drones on and on. Time seems to stretch like I’m watching a tortoise race. I think I’d rather watch paint dry, honestly. What was I talking about? Right, comfort zones. It’s so weird—every time I try to do something social I panic a little inside but at the same time feel this weird pull, like I should be out there making friends, but then I think, Why? I’ve got my books and my shows—what else do I need? People are complicated, and I don’t really want to wade through all that. Relationships feel like a big messy blob of expectations. Like, do I really want to get asked about my feelings? I can’t even keep my own feelings straight half the time. Jumping from anxiety about money to existential dread about climate change then back to the mundane worry that my laundry is piling up is exhausting. It’s like my brain is a happy little rollercoaster of chaos that never stops. Sometimes I just wish I could turn it off, unplug it, you know? I wonder what life would be like if I could just be normal—whatever normal is. I see people walking hand in hand and I’m like, ‘how do they do that?’ Are they happy? Or are they also thinking about their laundry piling up or their classes they forgot to attend? Maybe I should ask someone but then what if they start talking about their lives and I can’t relate? Small talk feels like swimming in a pool of slime. Then there’s the whole thing about ambition. Friends have plans, like big plans for the future, and I’m just sitting here like, “What’s my next binge-watch?” I think I have a fear of ambition, like it’s something I’m supposed to care about but I really don’t care if I’m not rich or famous or whatever. Maybe I should be a writer. But then I’d have to get out there and pitch stuff to people and I can’t even send an email without sweating. Emails are basically a social interaction in disguise. Why is it so hard to just communicate? Oh gosh, that reminds me of my last group project. No one wanted to do anything, and I just couldn’t bring myself to take the lead. I was just waiting for someone to take charge. I don’t want to tell people what to do, and they don’t want to listen to me anyway, so what’s the point? I ended up just doing the whole paper myself and not even caring that it was due the next day. I seriously have no idea how I passed. Maybe they just felt pity. That’s a thing, right? Pity grades? Then there are the late-night scrolls through Instagram. It’s like a black hole of curated lives that don’t reflect anything real. Everyone looks so perfect, and I think, yeah right, probably all photoshopped. It’s even worse when friends send me photos of their lives and I think, “Why is it so easy for them?” I feel like a spectator even in my own life, like I’m watching it unfold but not really participating. I sometimes feel like the background character in a sitcom that never gets a storyline. Is that just me? And the irony is I’m super open to new ideas and experiences. I’ll read anything, try any new food, watch whatever movies people suggest. But if it comes to, like, leaving my house? Nope. I’ll just find the nearest streaming service and call it a night. I mean, have you tried popcorn with sriracha? It’s a game changer. I found that out all on my own, which is cool, right? But what does it even mean to be “open” if you don’t use it to meet people? I get so wrapped up in my own thoughts that I forget the world is out there. Sometimes I even feel guilty about it. Do I have a responsibility to engage with the world? If I’m busy being creative, does that count? It’s like this constant tug-of-war between wanting to be alone and feeling bad about it. And then the guilt creeps in, and the cycle continues…

So I was thinking about how the coffee I had this morning was actually really good but now I’m craving another cup, even though I just had one, can you really have too much caffeine? I mean it’s just this nice warm feeling that spreads through your body, like a hug, and who doesn’t love a good hug? I was chatting with Jess earlier, she’s so upbeat, and her energy is contagious, we were brainstorming ideas for that group project, which I really want to knock out of the park, because honestly, collaborative work can either be a total disaster or just a blast, and I know we’re leaning into the blast side of things. It’s like when we all get together, the ideas just flow and everyone’s bouncing thoughts around, and I love how we all just respect each other’s input. I can picture the end result already, the presentation will be fun and interactive, maybe we can throw in some polls and quizzes, people love quizzes, right? Anyway, did I mention how I ran into Jamie yesterday at the park? Oh man, it felt like ages since I saw her, and we ended up talking for like an hour about everything under the sun. We laughed so hard about that time we tried to cook together and nearly burned the place down. Why do I always seem to forget how bad I am in the kitchen? I have friends who are such foodies and can whip up gourmet meals in no time, and I’m just over here burning toast. I should really take a cooking class or something just to keep it interesting and expand my skills. Plus, the idea of meeting new people in a cooking class sounds fun, just a bunch of folks chopping vegetables and chatting about life, unique flavors, and all that. I could definitely use a little more spice in my culinary repertoire. Speaking of spice, I’ve always wanted to go to that new Latin dance class they have downtown, I hear it’s lively and full of energy, and I know I can get lost in that rhythm. I love dancing, it’s freeing, and there’s just something about moving your body that really lifts my spirits. Sometimes I just turn on music in my room and let loose, but it’s not quite the same as being out there with other people, right? The energy in a room full of dancers is like electric. Just thinking about it makes me smile. My friends and I were joking about starting our own little dance crew just for kicks, but who knows, maybe we could even perform somewhere for fun. My roommate has this obsession with organizing everything, and I really admire that, I can be a little chaotic sometimes, like my side of the room is always messier, but it doesn’t bother me too much, I can find what I need when I need it, but she’s taught me that a little organization can go a long way. So I’ve been trying to be better about it, we even did a weekend declutter session, and you know what? It felt so satisfying. The idea of having space to breathe, without all that clutter around, it’s refreshing! I wonder if all those little changes we’re making are a sign of growing up or just trying to be better versions of ourselves, probably a mix of both. Also, I’ve been reading this book that’s just blowing my mind, it’s about different cultures and how they approach happiness. What a fascinating way to think about life. It’s like we’re all chasing the same thing yet, we go about it in such varying ways. I love learning about different perspectives; it opens your mind, you know? We could all use a little more of that. I’ve thought about starting a book club but something that focuses on discussion and not just critique—like diving deep into ideas and sharing personal experiences that relate to them. Oh! And I can’t believe I forgot to mention the concert coming up! I’m so pumped. I got tickets with a couple of friends, and I love live music, there’s nothing like being there, feeling the beat pulse through you, and just being surrounded by a crowd who’s vibing in the same way. The atmosphere is electric, the excitement! I hope they play my favorite song, it’s definitely a crowd-pleaser, and everyone’s energy just skyrockets when it comes on. Life just feels so full right now, like every day is a new adventure waiting to happen. It’s not just the big events either, sometimes the little moments spark joy too, like a random compliment from a stranger or a shared laugh during a lecture. I feel lucky to have so many great people around me, and I want to make the most of my time with them. Let’s create memories and explore the world together. It’s all about those connections, right? I just want to keep moving forward, keep learning and growing, and soaking it all in.

Okay so I keep thinking about that conversation I had the other night with Sam at the party. Like, how did we go from talking about the weather to debating if aliens exist? It’s always wild how fast those things can spiral. I kind of love it but also it gets chaotic. My head races when I think about it. Like, I can’t wrap my mind around how some people are so chill about their beliefs. I want to challenge everything, you know? But it’s not even just the aliens—it’s like, what’s the point of discussing if we can’t come to a conclusion? Most people just nod and say, “Yeah, sure” and go with it. I’m like, no, don't you care? The way Sam was talking about how openness is overrated—he thinks being critical and skeptical is the way to go. I mean, I guess he’s got a point. I can't really stand those folks who pretend to get along with everyone, just agree and smile. So fake. People are so exhausting sometimes. Maybe that’s what I like about parties—there's so much energy, so much going on. I can dive into conversations with new people and forget about how draining it can be when everyone’s just pretending to be nice. I’ve never been one to hold back. But it’s not like I want to be rude. Just straightforward. Why waste time? That’s what I think. Sometimes I worry too much about what others think of me, though, which is an annoying contradiction in my brain. I can love the spotlight but then I’m pacing in my room the day after a big event, convincing myself I said something dumb or offended someone. My mind races just thinking about it. I remember this one time I made a joke and nobody laughed. Crickets, seriously. I felt this heat rush to my face, and I had to change the subject quick. Man, social situations can be so stressful. And they’re supposed to be fun? But then I remember the way people laugh when I nail a story. It’s like a drug. Ah, the highs and lows. I don’t even know what to do with myself sometimes. I start a million things and can’t finish a single one. Like, I signed up for that dance class, and I was pumped for a week but then just bailed. I don't know, I’d rather be out, meeting people than stuck in one place. But maybe that’s what gets me into trouble. Last week, I promised I’d help my roommate study for his exam, and then I totally forgot. Caught up in my own world again. I should be more reliable. But I’m just not wired that way, I guess. My phone is always buzzing; I’m always texting someone on a whim. Then I think about how I just can’t deal with long-term plans. They always stress me out. I don’t want to be tied down by schedules and expectations. I mean, if there’s a party or a last-minute hangout, I'm down. Just drop everything and go! But a week from now? A month? Nah. Don’t even start with that. Some people love to plan everything, birthdays, vacations, and I just can't. I like spontaneity! But sometimes I wonder if it’s a good thing or not. Maybe if I was more organized, I wouldn’t be so frazzled. Then again, that’s also part of why I love being around others. They fill in those gaps, team up with me so I don’t feel so scattered. I thrive in crowd dynamics. I can be the life of the party or just that one loud voice in the corner. I don’t mind. I get the attention, and that’s enough most days. But I also think about friendships—how complicated they can get. People get offended, they want support, and I’m just not the best at it. I can be blunt, and I know I hurt feelings sometimes. But that’s just me. It’s hard for me to hear whining. Just get over it already, right? And then I spiral into my own worries again, like am I going to end up alone because I can’t relate to others on that emotional level? I need to find balance, I guess. It’s one thing to be outgoing and another to connect deeply. I can talk a big game but then I'm the first to back away when it gets heavy. There’s no handbook on this stuff—not that I’d read it anyway. I’m not into self-help books. I’d rather figure it out as I go. But maybe I need to think about being a bit more chilled out. Not be so neurotic about it. But who knows? That sounds boring. I’m not into boring. Okay I think I’m running out of steam now. Anyway, I want to be at that party this weekend. I just hope I don’t make a fool out of myself again. But also, that’s kind of the fun, right? Just rolling with it.

Okay, so I just remembered that one time I talked my way into a free concert because I was super chatty with the bouncer, just threw out some random facts about the band, and suddenly I was in. Makes me think: why can’t everyday life be like that? Like just walk up to people and convince them to give you things. I should’ve been a salesperson or something, but nah, too much small talk. I like big conversations, ideas bouncing. People are so weird, you know? Like how some will stand in a line for hours because of a new phone release. I mean, seriously, it’s a phone! Just wait a week and get it online. But that sense of community is interesting, isn’t it? Everyone together, buzzing with excitement like bees—I love that energy. But I’m also thinking of the last time I was in a crowded space and just felt overwhelmed. So many voices, so much noise, like my brain short-circuits. I guess that’s the neuroticism coming in, huh? Always on alert, ready to spiral into panic. Speaking of panic, I get anxious when I don’t have a plan. Like, what am I going to do today? It’s like a ticking clock in my head; if I don’t fill the day with something productive, I’ll lose my mind. I mean, I have to clean my apartment, but do I really want to? I’ve got laundry piled up, and every time I think about it, I just want to throw a blanket over it and pretend it doesn’t exist. I hate mess but the mess is so comforting in its familiarity. I love it when I have my own little organized chaos. Oh, I should check my planner; I tried bullet journaling last semester—it didn’t last long, but it was colorful. I bought all these fancy pens and stickers, the kind that just scream, “I’m organized!” but, really, I just wrote “Do laundry” in every box and skipped half my to-do’s because I got sidetracked by TikTok or something. And TikTok is a rabbit hole, I swear. I can’t stop scrolling through random recipes, outfit hacks, life advice that’s good in theory but terrible in practice. Like, who has the energy to cook elaborate meals every day? I just want to eat and get on with my life. Okay, so I tried the whipped coffee, what’s that called, dalgona or something? It tastes great, but honestly, all that whisking? My arm was sore for days. But it was fun to post on my story. I love sharing those little bits of life—even if they’re mundane, it’s like looking for connections. I want to be relatable but also inject my personality into everything: loud, unpredictable, always talking. Then again, I think about how many people just want to keep their heads down and avoid conflict. I get that, but it drives me crazy. Why not express yourself? Why not speak up? And yes, I know I can come off too strong sometimes, not everyone wants to hear my hot take on that new Netflix show or my fascination with the psychology of decisions. But isn’t it interesting how some people just nod along and don’t share their thoughts? It’s like I’m in a constant debate with myself about the balance between being polite and being honest. And I feel like I land on the honesty side. Maybe that’s why I’m not so great at relationships. I can’t help but say what’s on my mind, and sometimes that’s not what people want to hear. Oh, I got sidetracked. Back to being busy. Being conscientious, I like to stay on top of things, but like, too many obligations? I started a new project, this podcast with some friends, and now there’s so much to organize. We have a theme every week, and I love brainstorming those topics, but sometimes I just want to do something crazy and random. One time, I suggested we analyze the psychology behind different snack preferences, and they looked at me like I grew another head. Really? It’s fascinating! Like what does your choice of snack say about your personality? Mine says I’m a chaotic mix of sweet and salty, just like life. So, plans balance out with spontaneity, right? I crave adventure, but also structure? What does that even mean? I haven't figured it out. I want to travel, meet new people, but then there’s the fear of change. Everything's constantly shifting—friends move away, things change. I keep thinking about getting a train ticket and just going somewhere. Somewhere new where no one knows me; maybe that would help. The thrill of the unknown, and I’m buzzing with ideas, ready to go. But, then I remember I have responsibilities, papers to write, classes to attend.
